title: Jainism

infobox.value: ([/ˈdʒeɪnɪzəm, ˈdʒaɪnɪzəm/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*JAY-niz-əm, JYE-niz-əm*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")),[[1]](#cite_note-1) also known as **Jain Dharma**,[[2]](#cite_note-2) is an [Indian religion](/wiki/Indian_religion "Indian religion") which teaches a path toward spiritual purity and enlightenment through disciplined non-violence (**[ahimsa](/wiki/Ahimsa_in_Jainism "Ahimsa in Jainism")**) to all living creatures. The tradition is spiritually guided by twenty-four **[tirthankaras](/wiki/Tirthankara "Tirthankara")** (ford-makers), supreme teachers who have conquered the cycle of rebirth and attained omniscience (**[kevala jnana](/wiki/Kevala_jnana "Kevala jnana")**). The core of [Jain philosophy](/wiki/Jain_philosophy "Jain philosophy") is established on three ethical pillars: **ahiṃsā** (nonviolence), **[anekāntavāda](/wiki/Anek%C4%81ntav%C4%81da "Anekāntavāda")** (non-absolutism or many-sided reality), and **aparigraha** ([non-possession](/wiki/Non-possession "Non-possession")). While its ultimate spiritual goal is **[moksha](/wiki/Moksha_(Jainism) "Moksha (Jainism)")** (liberation from **[karma](/wiki/Karma_in_Jainism "Karma in Jainism")**), these ethical principles have historically fostered a community renowned for its high literacy, trusted role in commerce, and distinct intellectual culture.

(non-attachment) requires [monks](/wiki/Jain_monasticism "Jain monasticism") to renounce all property, while encouraging laypersons to limit their possessions and voluntarily limit their desires (**iccha-parimana**). Historically, the application of non-violence drove the [Jain community](/wiki/Jain_community "Jain community") away from agriculture and warfare toward trade and banking, where they became a dominant mercantile force in ancient and medieval India, supporting a vast network of [temples](/wiki/Jain_temple "Jain temple"), libraries, and charitable institutions.

guiding every cosmic time cycle. In the current cycle, the first **tirthankara** was *[Rishabhanatha](/wiki/Rishabhanatha "Rishabhanatha")*, credited in tradition with establishing civilized society. The 23rd **tirthankara**, [Pārśvanātha](/wiki/P%C4%81r%C5%9Bvan%C4%81tha "Pārśvanātha"), is dated by historians to the c. 8th or 7th century BCE, making him likely the earliest historical figure of the tradition. The 24th and final **tirthankara**, [Mahavira](/wiki/Mahavira "Mahavira") c. 6th or 5th century BCE, was a contemporary of the [Buddha](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha") and a central figure in the **[Śramaṇa](/wiki/%C5%9Arama%E1%B9%87a "Śramaṇa")** movement of [Greater Magadha](/wiki/Greater_Magadha "Greater Magadha"), which rejected the authority of the Vedas and established the current ascetic order.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBronkhorst200714-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

or **Jainas**, residing mostly in [India](/wiki/India "India"), with significant diaspora communities in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), and [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"). The community is divided into two major sub-traditions, the **[Digambaras](/wiki/Digambara "Digambara")** ("sky-clad") and **[Śvētāmbaras](/wiki/%C5%9Av%C4%93t%C4%81mbara "Śvētāmbara")** ("white-clad"), which differ on ascetic practices, gender, and canonical texts, though they share the same core philosophy. Despite their small numbers, Jains have exerted a disproportionate influence on [Indian culture](/wiki/Indian_culture "Indian culture"), contributing significantly to the development of logic, art, architecture, and the legal and ethical frameworks of modern India. Major festivals include **[Paryushana](/wiki/Paryushana "Paryushana")** or **das lakshana**, **[Akshaya Tritiya](/wiki/Akshaya_Tritiya "Akshaya Tritiya")**, **[Mahavir Janma Kalyanak](/wiki/Mahavir_Janma_Kalyanak "Mahavir Janma Kalyanak")**, and **[Diwali](/wiki/Diwali_(Jainism) "Diwali (Jainism)")**.

**Jainism** ([/ˈdʒeɪnɪzəm, ˈdʒaɪnɪzəm/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*JAY-niz-əm, JYE-niz-əm*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")),[[1]](#cite_note-1) also known as **Jain Dharma**,[[2]](#cite_note-2) is an [Indian religion](/wiki/Indian_religion "Indian religion") which teaches a path toward spiritual purity and enlightenment through disciplined non-violence (**[ahimsa](/wiki/Ahimsa_in_Jainism "Ahimsa in Jainism")**) to all living creatures. The tradition is spiritually guided by twenty-four **[tirthankaras](/wiki/Tirthankara "Tirthankara")** (ford-makers), supreme teachers who have conquered the cycle of rebirth and attained omniscience (**[kevala jnana](/wiki/Kevala_jnana "Kevala jnana")**). The core of [Jain philosophy](/wiki/Jain_philosophy "Jain philosophy") is established on three ethical pillars: **ahiṃsā** (nonviolence), **[anekāntavāda](/wiki/Anek%C4%81ntav%C4%81da "Anekāntavāda")** (non-absolutism or many-sided reality), and **aparigraha** ([non-possession](/wiki/Non-possession "Non-possession")). While its ultimate spiritual goal is **[moksha](/wiki/Moksha_(Jainism) "Moksha (Jainism)")** (liberation from **[karma](/wiki/Karma_in_Jainism "Karma in Jainism")**), these ethical principles have historically fostered a community renowned for its high literacy, trusted role in commerce, and distinct intellectual culture.

Jain philosophy distinguishes itself through the doctrine of **anekāntavāda**, which asserts that truth and reality are complex and always have multiple aspects; thus, no single viewpoint can claim absolute truth. This framework encourages intellectual humility and conflict resolution, contrasting with the "one-sided" (**ekānta**) views rejected by the tradition. Ethically, the vow of **aparigraha** (non-attachment) requires [monks](/wiki/Jain_monasticism "Jain monasticism") to renounce all property, while encouraging laypersons to limit their possessions and voluntarily limit their desires (**iccha-parimana**). Historically, the application of non-violence drove the [Jain community](/wiki/Jain_community "Jain community") away from agriculture and warfare toward trade and banking, where they became a dominant mercantile force in ancient and medieval India, supporting a vast network of [temples](/wiki/Jain_temple "Jain temple"), libraries, and charitable institutions.

The tradition views itself as eternal, with the **tirthankaras** guiding every cosmic time cycle. In the current cycle, the first **tirthankara** was *[Rishabhanatha](/wiki/Rishabhanatha "Rishabhanatha")*, credited in tradition with establishing civilized society. The 23rd **tirthankara**, [Pārśvanātha](/wiki/P%C4%81r%C5%9Bvan%C4%81tha "Pārśvanātha"), is dated by historians to the c. 8th or 7th century BCE, making him likely the earliest historical figure of the tradition. The 24th and final **tirthankara**, [Mahavira](/wiki/Mahavira "Mahavira") c. 6th or 5th century BCE, was a contemporary of the [Buddha](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha") and a central figure in the **[Śramaṇa](/wiki/%C5%9Arama%E1%B9%87a "Śramaṇa")** movement of [Greater Magadha](/wiki/Greater_Magadha "Greater Magadha"), which rejected the authority of the Vedas and established the current ascetic order.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBronkhorst200714-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Jainism has between four and five million followers, known as **Jains** or **Jainas**, residing mostly in [India](/wiki/India "India"), with significant diaspora communities in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), and [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"). The community is divided into two major sub-traditions, the **[Digambaras](/wiki/Digambara "Digambara")** ("sky-clad") and **[Śvētāmbaras](/wiki/%C5%9Av%C4%93t%C4%81mbara "Śvētāmbara")** ("white-clad"), which differ on ascetic practices, gender, and canonical texts, though they share the same core philosophy. Despite their small numbers, Jains have exerted a disproportionate influence on [Indian culture](/wiki/Indian_culture "Indian culture"), contributing significantly to the development of logic, art, architecture, and the legal and ethical frameworks of modern India. Major festivals include **[Paryushana](/wiki/Paryushana "Paryushana")** or **das lakshana**, **[Akshaya Tritiya](/wiki/Akshaya_Tritiya "Akshaya Tritiya")**, **[Mahavir Janma Kalyanak](/wiki/Mahavir_Janma_Kalyanak "Mahavir Janma Kalyanak")**, and **[Diwali](/wiki/Diwali_(Jainism) "Diwali (Jainism)")**.

## Etymology

The name *Jainism* comes from *ji* (Sanskrit), "to conquer," referring to the battle against the passions and bodily desires, aiming to reach omniscience. Those few who accomplish this are called *jina*, "conqueror." Adherents are called *jain* or *jaina*,[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUpinder_Singh2024313-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESangave200615-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) "follower of the conquerors," a term which replaced the older name *nirgrantha*, bondless, which was only used for ascetic wanderers.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShahDundas-8)

## Jain doctrine and philosophy

Main article: [Jain philosophy](/wiki/Jain_philosophy "Jain philosophy")

### *Ratnatraya* (the three jewels)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Ahimsa_Jainism.svg/250px-Ahimsa_Jainism.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ahimsa_Jainism.svg)

The hand symbolizes [*Ahiṃsā*](/wiki/Ahimsa_in_Jainism "Ahimsa in Jainism"), the wheel *[dharmachakra](/wiki/Dharmachakra "Dharmachakra")*, the resolve to halt *saṃsāra* (transmigration).

Main articles: [Moksha (Jainism)](/wiki/Moksha_(Jainism) "Moksha (Jainism)"), [Ratnatraya](/wiki/Ratnatraya "Ratnatraya"), and [Gunasthana](/wiki/Gunasthana "Gunasthana")

Its salvational goal for ascetics is to reach *[moksha](/wiki/Moksha_(Jainism) "Moksha (Jainism)")* (liberation), while for most Jain laypersons it is to accumulate good *[karma](/wiki/Karma "Karma")* that leads to better rebirth and a step closer to liberation.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBailey2012108-9)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong201318,_98–100-10) Purification of soul and liberation can be achieved through the three jewels (*ratnatraya*):[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a6–7-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFohr20159–10,_37-12) *samyak darśana*, right vision or faith in the Jain teachings[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFohr201510-13) or scriptures,[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes1996323-14) more specifically the seven tattvas;[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes1996279-15)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998148,_151,_200-16)[[a]](#cite_note-18) *samyak gyana*, right knowledge and understanding of the Jain teachings,[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes1996323-14)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a7-19) more specifically of self (*jiva*) and non-self (*ajiva*);[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes1996279-15) and *samyak charitra*, correct conduct, behavior consistent with these teachinngs and the five vows.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes1996323-14)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998148,_200-20) Jain texts often add *samyak tapas* (correct asceticism) as a fourth jewel, emphasizing belief in ascetic practices as the means to liberation ([moksha](/wiki/Moksha "Moksha")).[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a7-19) The four jewels are called *Moksha Marga* (the path of liberation).[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a6–7-11)

The core of Jainism is also summarized in the three tenets of **[ahiṃsā](/wiki/Ahimsa_in_Jainism "Ahimsa in Jainism")** (nonviolence), **[aparigraha](/wiki/Aparigraha "Aparigraha")** (asceticism), and **[anekāntavāda](/wiki/Anek%C4%81ntav%C4%81da "Anekāntavāda")**, a rejection of all simplistic and one-sided views of truth and reality.

### Core principles (the vows)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Nishidhi_stone_with_14th_century_Old_Kannada_inscription_from_Tavanandi_forest.JPG/250px-Nishidhi_stone_with_14th_century_Old_Kannada_inscription_from_Tavanandi_forest.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nishidhi_stone_with_14th_century_Old_Kannada_inscription_from_Tavanandi_forest.JPG)

[Nishidhi stone](/wiki/Doddahundi_nishidhi_inscription "Doddahundi nishidhi inscription"), depicting the vow of *sallekhana*, 14th century, [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka")

Main article: [Ethics of Jainism](/wiki/Ethics_of_Jainism "Ethics of Jainism")

See also: [Yamas § Five Yamas](/wiki/Yamas#Five_Yamas "Yamas")

Jainism teaches five ethical duties, which it calls five vows. These are called *anuvratas* (small vows) for Jain laypersons, and *mahavratas* (great vows) for Jain mendicants.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925228–231-21) For both, its moral precepts preface that the Jain has access to a *[guru](/wiki/Guru "Guru")* (teacher, counsellor), *deva* (Jina, god), doctrine, and that the individual is free from five offences: doubts about the faith, indecisiveness about the truths of Jainism, insincerity of desire for Jain teachings, non-recognition of fellow Jains, and insufficient admiration of fellow Jains' spiritual endeavors.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925228-22) Such a person undertakes the following five vows of Jainism:

1. *[Ahiṃsā](/wiki/Ahi%E1%B9%83s%C4%81 "Ahiṃsā")*, "intentional non-violence" or "noninjury":[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925228-22) The first major vow taken by Jains is to cause no harm to other human beings, as well as all living beings (particularly animals).[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925228-22) This is the highest ethical duty in Jainism, and it applies not only to one's actions, but demands that one be non-violent in one's speech and thoughts.[[22]](#cite_note-pkshah5v-23)[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVijay_K._Jain201233-24)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998170–173-25)
2. *[Satya](/wiki/Satya "Satya")*, "truth": This vow is to always speak the truth. Neither lie, nor speak what is not true, and do not encourage others or approve anyone who speaks an untruth.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925228–231-21)[[22]](#cite_note-pkshah5v-23)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998173–175-26)
3. *[Asteya](/wiki/Asteya "Asteya")*, "not stealing": A Jain layperson should not take anything that is not willingly given.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925228-22)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVijay_K._Jain201268-27) Additionally, a Jain mendicant should ask for permission to take it if something is being given.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925231-28)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998175-29)
4. *[Brahmacharya](/wiki/Brahmacharya "Brahmacharya")*, "celibacy": Abstinence from sex and sensual pleasures is prescribed for Jain monks and nuns. For laypersons, the vow means chastity, faithfulness to one's partner.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925228–231-21)[[22]](#cite_note-pkshah5v-23)[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998176–177-30)
5. *[Aparigraha](/wiki/Aparigraha "Aparigraha")*, "non-possessiveness": This includes non-attachment to material and psychological possessions, avoiding craving and greed.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925228–231-21) Jain monks and nuns completely renounce property and social relations, own nothing and are attached to no one.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah2004112-31)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2013109-32)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998177–178-33)

Jainism prescribes seven supplementary vows, including three *guņa vratas* (merit vows) and four *śikşā vratas*.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVijay_K._Jain201287–88-34)[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETukol19765-35) The *[Sallekhana](/wiki/Sallekhana "Sallekhana")* (or *Santhara*) vow is a "religious death" ritual observed at the end of life, historically by Jain monks and nuns, but rare in the modern age.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002179–180-36) In this vow, there is voluntary and gradual reduction of food and liquid intake to end one's life by choice and with dispassion,[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini200016-37)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETukol19767-38) This is believed to reduce negative karma that affects a soul's future rebirths.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1991166–167-39)

#### *Ahimsa* – non-violence

Main article: [Ahimsa in Jainism](/wiki/Ahimsa_in_Jainism "Ahimsa in Jainism")

The principle of *[ahimsa](/wiki/Ahimsa_in_Jainism "Ahimsa in Jainism")* (non-violence or non-injury) is a fundamental tenet of Jainism.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002160-40) It holds that one must abandon all violent activity and that without such a commitment to non-violence all religious behavior is worthless.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002160-40) In Jain theology, it does not matter how correct or defensible the violence may be, one must not kill or harm any being, and non-violence is the highest religious duty.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002160-40)[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarkhamLohr200971-41) Jain texts such as *[Ācārāṅga Sūtra](/wiki/%C4%80c%C4%81r%C4%81%E1%B9%85ga_S%C5%ABtra "Ācārāṅga Sūtra")* and *[Tattvarthasūtra](/wiki/Tattvartha_Sutra "Tattvartha Sutra")* state that one must renounce all killing of living beings, whether tiny or large, movable or immovable.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice201090-42)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002160–162-43) Its theology teaches that one must neither kill another living being, nor cause another to kill, nor consent to any killing directly or indirectly.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarkhamLohr200971-41)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice201090-42)

Furthermore, Jainism emphasizes non-violence against all beings not only in action but also in speech and in thought.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice201090-42)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002160–162-43) It states that instead of hate or violence against anyone, "all living creatures must help each other".[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002160–162-43)[[b]](#cite_note-45)

Jains believe that violence negatively affects and destroys one's soul, particularly when the violence is done with intent, hate or carelessness, or when one indirectly causes or consents to the killing of a human or non-human living being.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002160–162-43)

The doctrine exists in Hinduism and Buddhism, but is most highly developed in Jainism.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002160-40)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundararajanMukherji1997392–417-46)[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIzawa200878–81-47)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESethia20042-48)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWinternitz1993409-49) The theological basis of non-violence as the highest religious duty has been interpreted by some Jain scholars not to "be driven by merit from giving or compassion to other creatures, nor a duty to rescue all creatures", but resulting from "continual self-discipline", a cleansing of the soul that leads to one's own spiritual development which ultimately affects one's salvation and release from rebirths.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200288–89,_257–258-50) Jains believe that causing injury to any being in any form creates bad [karma](/wiki/Karma_in_Jainism "Karma in Jainism") which affects one's rebirth, future well-being and causes suffering.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor2008892–894-51)[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranoff1992-52)

Late medieval Jain scholars re-examined the *Ahiṃsā* doctrine when faced with external threat or violence. For example, they justified violence by monks to protect nuns.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002162–163-53)[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELorenzen197861–75-54) According to [Dundas](/wiki/Paul_Dundas "Paul Dundas"), the Jain scholar [Jinadattasuri](/wiki/Jinadattasuri "Jinadattasuri") wrote during a time of destruction of temples and persecution that "anybody engaged in a religious activity who was forced to fight and kill somebody would not lose any spiritual merit but instead attain deliverance".[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002163-55)

However, examples in Jain texts that condone fighting and killing under certain circumstances are relatively rare.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002162–163-53)[[c]](#cite_note-57)

#### *Aparigraha* – non-attachment

Main article: [Aparigraha](/wiki/Aparigraha "Aparigraha")

The third main principle in Jainism is *aparigraha* which means non-attachment to worldly possessions.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah2004112-31) For monks and nuns, Jainism requires a vow of complete non-possession of any property, relations and emotions.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002117,_152-58) The ascetic is a wandering mendicant in the Digambara tradition, or a resident mendicant in the [Śvētāmbara](/wiki/%C5%9Av%C4%93t%C4%81mbara "Śvētāmbara") tradition.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002117,_152-58) For Jain laypersons, it recommends limited possession of property that has been honestly earned, and giving excess property to charity.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah2004112-31) According to Natubhai Shah, *aparigraha* applies to both the material and the psychic. Material possessions refer to various forms of property. Psychic possessions refer to emotions, likes and dislikes, and attachments of any form. Unchecked attachment to possessions is said to result in direct harm to one's personality.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah2004112–113-59)

### Metaphysics and cosmology

Main article: [Jain cosmology](/wiki/Jain_cosmology "Jain cosmology")

Jain metaphysics explains the nature of the universe and its interaction with the soul (*jiva*).[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong201383–85-60) It posits that the soul is an eternal entity that is separate from the body and the physical world.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong201383–85-60) Their interaction is what defines the cycle of rebirth (*Saṃsāra*).[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong201383–85-60)

#### Soul and karma

Main article: [Karma in Jainism](/wiki/Karma_in_Jainism "Karma in Jainism")

According to Jain beliefs, vibrational energy (*virya*) draws [karmic particles](/wiki/Karma_in_Jainism "Karma in Jainism") to the soul and creates bondages.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998104–106-61) Purification of soul from karmic particles and thereby liberation can be achieved through *[ratnatraya](/wiki/Ratnatraya "Ratnatraya")*, the path of the three jewels,[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a6–7-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFohr20159–10,_37-12) namely *samyak darśana*, correct faith in the Jain teachings; *samyak gyana*, correct knowledge and understanding of those teachings; and *samyak charitra*, correct conduct, behavior consistent with the five vows.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998148,_200-20)[[d]](#cite_note-62)

Jainism, like other Indian religions, believes in *karma*, but with a unique and fundamental difference. It is the only tradition that conceives of *karma* as a physical, material substance — subtle, invisible particles of matter (*pudgala*) that exist in the universe.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998104–106-61) These particles are drawn to the soul (*jiva*) by its actions, thoughts, and words.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998103-63)

This "*karmic* dirt" then sticks to the soul, obscuring its innate, pure qualities of consciousness and bliss.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925194-64)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong201392–95-65) This bondage of *karma* is the cause of the soul's entrapment in *Saṃsāra* and its repeated cycles of birth and death.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200299–103-66)

#### The *Tattvas* (fundamental truths)

Main article: [Tattva (Jainism)](/wiki/Tattva_(Jainism) "Tattva (Jainism)")

The *tattvas* are the seven (or nine) fundamental truths that form the basis of the entire Jain path. They describe the step-by-step process of *karmic* bondage and liberation:[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925188–190-67)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980219–228-68)

1. *[Jīva](/wiki/J%C4%ABva_(Jainism) "Jīva (Jainism)")* — The living, sentient soul.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925188–190-67)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980219–228-68)
2. *[Ajīva](/wiki/Aj%C4%ABva "Ajīva")* — Non-living substances, including matter, time, and *karma*.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925188–190-67)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980219–228-68)
3. *[Āsrava](/wiki/%C4%80srava "Āsrava")* — The influx or inflow of *karmic* particles to the soul.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925188–190-67)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980219–228-68)
4. *[Bandha](/wiki/Bandha_(Jainism) "Bandha (Jainism)")* — The bondage of these *karmic* particles to the soul.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925188–190-67)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980219–228-68)
5. *[Saṃvara](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%83vara "Saṃvara")* — The stoppage of new *karmic* inflow (achieved by vows and discipline).[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925188–190-67)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980219–228-68)
6. *[Nirjarā](/wiki/Nirjar%C4%81 "Nirjarā")* — The shedding or purification of existing, bound *karma* (achieved by asceticism).[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925188–190-67)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980219–228-68)
7. *[Moksha](/wiki/Moksha "Moksha")* — The complete liberation of the soul, freeing it from all *karma* to regain its pure, omniscient state.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925188–190-67)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980219–228-68)

Some texts add two more categories, *Punya* (good *karma*) and *Paapa* (bad *karma*), as sub-types of *Āsrava* and *Bandha*, making nine *tattvas* in total.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925177–187-69)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998151-70)[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200296–98-71)

#### *Saṃsāra* (cycle of rebirth)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Jiva.jpg/250px-Jiva.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jiva.jpg)

Classification of *Saṃsāri Jīvas* (transmigrating souls) in Jainism

Main articles: [Saṃsāra (Jainism)](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%83s%C4%81ra_(Jainism) "Saṃsāra (Jainism)") and [Vitalism (Jainism)](/wiki/Vitalism_(Jainism) "Vitalism (Jainism)")

*Saṃsāra* is the doctrine of the worldly cycle of birth, death, and rebirth, through which the soul transmigrates based on its *karma*.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980227–228-72) This cycle is considered the natural state of existence, but it is also one of suffering, and the ultimate aim of Jainism is liberation (*Moksha*) from it.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980224–225-73)[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESethia200430–31-74)

According to Jain tradition, souls can be reborn in one of four states of existence (*gatis*): as heavenly beings, humans, animals/plants, or hellish beings.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980223–228-75) Jain theosophy also describes a vast number of potential birth-situations, traditionally 8.4 million, through which the unliberated soul cycles.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980223–228-75)[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini2000130–131-76) Jain philosophy also uniquely posits the existence of *abhavya* (incapable) souls, a category of souls that are eternally trapped in *Saṃsāra* and can never attain liberation.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980225–226-77)[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002104–105-78)

#### Cosmology: substance, time, and realms

Main article: [Dravya](/wiki/Dravya "Dravya")

Jain cosmology views the universe as an uncreated, eternal, and self-sustaining entity.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChampat_Rai_Jain191715-79) It was never created by a god and will never be destroyed.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChampat_Rai_Jain191715-79)

This universe is composed of six eternal substances known as *dravya*:

1. *Jīva* (the living soul)[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes1996118–119-80)[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes199664-65-81)[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200293-82)
2. *[Pudgala](/wiki/Pudgala "Pudgala")* (non-sentient matter)[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes1996118–119-80)[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes199664-65-81)[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200293-82)
3. *[Dharma](/wiki/Dharma_(Jainism) "Dharma (Jainism)")* (the principle of motion)[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes199664-65-81)[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200293-82)
4. *[Adharma](/wiki/Adharma "Adharma")* (the principle of rest)[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes199664-65-81)[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200293-82)
5. *[Ākāśa](/wiki/%C4%80k%C4%81%C5%9Ba_(Jainism) "Ākāśa (Jainism)")* (space)[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes1996118–119-80)[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes199664-65-81)[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200293-82)
6. *[Kāla](/wiki/K%C4%81la_(time) "Kāla (time)")* (time)[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes199664-65-81)[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200293-82)[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENemicandraBalbir20101_of_Introduction-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Jain_Cosmic_Time_Cycle.jpg/250px-Jain_Cosmic_Time_Cycle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jain_Cosmic_Time_Cycle.jpg)

Division of time in Jain cosmology

*Kāla* (time) is itself conceived as a boundless, eternal wheel (*kālachakra*) that rotates ceaselessly.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoniger1999551-84) It is divided into two half-cycles: an ascending arc (*utsarpiṇī*) of progressive happiness and virtue, and a descending arc (*avasarpiṇī*) of progressive sorrow and decline.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVijay_K._Jain201146-85)[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoniger1999551-84)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUpinder_Singh2024313-5)[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925271–272-86)[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200213-87)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChampat_Rai_Jain1929b124-88)[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a27-89)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Jain_universe.JPG/250px-Jain_universe.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jain_universe.JPG)

Rebirth *loka* (realms of existence) in Jain cosmology[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200290–92-90)

The universe itself is structurally divided into three realms, or *lokas*: the *Urdhva Loka* (upper world of heavenly beings), the *Madhya Loka* (middle world of humans, animals, and plants), and the *Adho Loka* (lower world of hellish beings).[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah199825-91) All unliberated souls, including gods and demons, transmigrate through these three realms based on their karma.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah199825-91)

### Epistemology

Main article: [Jain epistemology](/wiki/Jain_epistemology "Jain epistemology")

Jain philosophy accepts three reliable means of knowledge (*[pramana](/wiki/Pramana "Pramana")*). It holds that correct knowledge is based on perception (*pratyaksa*), inference (*anumana*), and testimony (*sabda* or the word of scriptures).[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimes1996238-92)[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESoni2000367–377-93) These ideas are elaborated in Jain texts such as *Tattvarthasūtra*, *Parvacanasara*, *Nandi* and *Anuyogadvarini*.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200275–76,_131,_229–230-94)[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESoni2000367–377-93) Some Jain texts add analogy (*upamana*) as the fourth reliable means, in a manner similar to [epistemological theories](/wiki/Pramana "Pramana") found in other Indian religions.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002229–230-95)

In Jainism, *jnāna* (knowledge) is said to be of five kinds—*mati jñāna* (sensory knowledge), *śrutu jñāna* (scriptural knowledge), *avadhi jñāna* ([clairvoyance](/wiki/Clairvoyance "Clairvoyance")), *manah prayāya Jñāna* ([telepathy](/wiki/Telepathy "Telepathy")) and *[kevala jnana](/wiki/Kevala_jnana "Kevala jnana")* ([omniscience](/wiki/Omniscience "Omniscience")).[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTES.A._Jain199216-96) According to the Jain text *Tattvartha sūtra*, the first two are indirect knowledge, and the remaining three are direct knowledge.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVijay_K._Jain20116-97)

#### *Anekāntavāda* - many-sided reality

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Medieval_Jain_temple_Anekantavada_doctrine_artwork.jpg/250px-Medieval_Jain_temple_Anekantavada_doctrine_artwork.jpg)](/wiki/File:Medieval_Jain_temple_Anekantavada_doctrine_artwork.jpg)

Jain temple painting explaining Anekantavada with [Blind men and an elephant](/wiki/Blind_men_and_an_elephant "Blind men and an elephant")

Main article: [Anekantavada](/wiki/Anekantavada "Anekantavada")

The second main principle of Jainism is *anekāntavāda*,[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharitrapragya200475–79-98)[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002229–231-99) from *anekānta* ("many-sidedness," etymologically "[non](/wiki/Privative_a "Privative a")-oneness" or "not being one") and *vada* ("doctrine").[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharitrapragya200475–79-98)[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002229–231-99) The doctrine states that truth and reality are complex and always have multiple aspects. It further states that reality can be experienced, but cannot be fully expressed with language. It suggests that human attempts to communicate are *Naya*, "partial expression of the truth".[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharitrapragya200475–79-98) According to it, one can experience the taste of truth, but cannot fully express that taste through language. It holds that attempts to express experience are *syāt*, or valid "in some respect", but remain "perhaps, just one perspective, incomplete".[[96]](#cite_note-100) It concludes that in the same way, spiritual truths can be experienced but not fully expressed.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharitrapragya200475–79-98) It suggests that the great error is belief in *ekānta* (one-sidedness), where some relative truth is treated as absolute.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwartz2018-101) The doctrine is ancient, found in Buddhist texts such as the *Samaññaphala Sutta*. The Jain Agamas suggest that Mahāvīra's approach to answering all metaphysical philosophical questions was a "qualified yes" (*syāt*).[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatilal1990301–305-102)[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBalcerowicz2015205–218-103) These texts identify *anekāntavāda* as a key difference from the [Buddha](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha")'s teachings. The Buddha taught the Middle Way, rejecting extremes of the answer "it is" or "it is not" to metaphysical questions. The Mahāvīra, in contrast, taught his followers to accept both "it is", and "it is not", qualified with "perhaps", to understand Absolute Reality.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatilal1998128–135-104) The permanent being is conceptualized as *[jiva](/wiki/Jiva_(Jainism) "Jiva (Jainism)")* (soul) and *[ajiva](/wiki/Ajiva "Ajiva")* (matter) within a dualistic *anekāntavāda* framework.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200290–99,_104–105,_229–233-105)

According to [Paul Dundas](/wiki/Paul_Dundas "Paul Dundas"), in contemporary times the *anekāntavāda* doctrine has been interpreted by some Jains as intending to "promote a universal religious tolerance", and a teaching of "plurality" and "benign attitude to other [ethical, religious] positions". Dundas states this is a misreading of historical texts and Mahāvīra's teachings.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002232–234-106) According to him, the "many pointedness, multiple perspective" teachings of the Mahāvīra is about the nature of [absolute reality](/wiki/Absolute_(philosophy) "Absolute (philosophy)") and human existence.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESethia200486–91-107) He claims that it is not about condoning activities such as killing animals for food, nor violence against disbelievers or any other living being as "perhaps right".[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002232–234-106) The five vows for Jain monks and nuns, for example, are strict requirements and there is no "perhaps" about them.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong201398–106-108) Similarly, since ancient times, Jainism co-existed with Buddhism and Hinduism according to Dundas, but Jainism disagreed, in specific areas, with the knowledge systems and beliefs of these traditions, and vice versa.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002233-109)

### Concept of God and Tirthankaras

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Jain_24-Tirthankaras.jpg/250px-Jain_24-Tirthankaras.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jain_24-Tirthankaras.jpg)

Jain miniature painting of 24 tirthankaras, [Jaipur](/wiki/Jaipur "Jaipur"), c. 1850

Main article: [God in Jainism](/wiki/God_in_Jainism "God in Jainism")

Jainism is a [transtheistic](/wiki/Transtheistic "Transtheistic") religion,[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmer1953182-110) holding that the [universe was not created](/wiki/Jainism_and_non-creationism "Jainism and non-creationism"), and will exist forever.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925241-111) The universe is independent, having no creator, governor, judge, or destroyer.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong201383–85-60)[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925241–242-112) In this, it is unlike the Abrahamic religions and the theistic strands of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), but similar to Buddhism.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925241–243-113) However, Jainism believes in the world of heavenly and hellish beings who are born, die, and reborn like earthly beings.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925247–249,_262–263-114)[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200220–21,_34–35,_74,_91,_95–96,_103-115) The souls who live happily in the body of a heavenly celestial do so because of their positive karma.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925262–263-116) It is further stated that they possess a more transcendent knowledge about material things and can anticipate events in the human realms.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925262–263-116) However, once their past karmic merit is exhausted, it is explained that their souls are reborn again as humans, animals, or other beings.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925262–263-116)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200291,_95–96-117) The perfect enlightened souls *with* a body are called *[Arihants](/wiki/Arihant_(Jainism) "Arihant (Jainism)")* (victors) and perfect souls *without* a body are called *[Siddhas](/wiki/Siddha "Siddha")* (liberated souls). Only a soul with a human body can attain enlightenment and liberation. The liberated beings are the supreme beings and are worshipped by all heavenly, earthly, and hellish beings who aspire to attain liberation themselves.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1980222–223-118)[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmer1953182-110)[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERankinMardia201340-119)

## History

Main article: [History of Jainism](/wiki/History_of_Jainism "History of Jainism")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Jainism](/wiki/Timeline_of_Jainism "Timeline of Jainism").

Jainism is a religion founded in [ancient India](/wiki/Ancient_India "Ancient India"). Jains trace their history through twenty-four *[tirthankaras](/wiki/Tirthankars "Tirthankars")* and revere [Rishabhanatha](/wiki/Rishabhanatha "Rishabhanatha") as the first *tirthankara* (in the present time-cycle). Some artifacts found in the [Indus River Valley civilization](/wiki/Indus_Valley_civilisation "Indus Valley civilisation") have been suggested as a link to ancient Jain culture, but very little is known about the Indus Valley iconography and script. The last two *tirthankaras*, the 23rd *tirthankara* [Parshvanatha](/wiki/Parshvanatha "Parshvanatha") (c. 9th–8th century BCE) and the 24th *tirthankara* [Mahavira](/wiki/Mahavira "Mahavira") (c. 599 – c. 527 BCE) are historical figures. Mahavira was a contemporary of the [Buddha](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha"). According to Jain texts, the 22nd *Tirthankara* [Neminatha](/wiki/Neminatha "Neminatha") lived about 85,000 years ago and was the cousin of [Krishna](/wiki/Krishna "Krishna").[[116]](#cite_note-120)

### Origins: Parshvanatha and Mahavira

See also: [Timeline of Jainism](/wiki/Timeline_of_Jainism "Timeline of Jainism") and [Śramaṇa](/wiki/%C5%9Arama%E1%B9%87a "Śramaṇa")

Jainism is an ancient [Indian religion](/wiki/Indian_religion "Indian religion") of obscure origins.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESangave2001185-121)[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERankinMardia2013975-122)[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp192513-123) Jains claim it to be eternal, and consider the first *tirthankara* [Rishabhanatha](/wiki/Rishabhanatha "Rishabhanatha") as the reinforcer of Jain Dharma in the current time cycle.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp192516-124) It is one of the *[Śramaṇa](/wiki/%C5%9Arama%E1%B9%87a "Śramaṇa")* traditions of ancient India, those that rejected the [Vedas](/wiki/Veda "Veda"),[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELochtefeld2002b639-125)[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBillimoria19881–30-126) and according to the twentieth-century scholar of comparative religion [Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan](/wiki/Sarvepalli_Radhakrishnan "Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan"), Jainism was in existence before the Vedas were composed.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJambuvijaya2002114-127)[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPande1957353-128)[[e]](#cite_note-130)

The first twenty two tirthankaras are not considered by non-Jain scholars as [historical](/wiki/Historicity "Historicity") figures.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESangave2001104,_129-131)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaraswati1908444-132) The 23rd Tirthankara, [Parshvanatha](/wiki/Parshvanatha "Parshvanatha"), was likely a historical being,[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmer1953183-133)[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini199810-134) dated by the Jain tradition to the ninth century BCE;[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarnett19577-135) historians date him to the eighth or seventh century BCE.[[131]](#cite_note-136) Parshvanatha may have founded a proto-Jain [ascetic](/wiki/%C5%9Arama%E1%B9%87a "Śramaṇa") community which subsequently got revived and reformed by [Mahavira](/wiki/Mahavira "Mahavira").[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBasham1951108-137)[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200230-31-138)

Mahāvīra is considered a contemporary of the Buddha, in around the sixth or 5th century BCE.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2003a383-139)[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeownPrebish2013127–130-140) The interaction between the two religions began with the Buddha;[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESangave2001105-141) later, they competed for followers and the merchant trade networks that sustained them.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeelis201072–76-142)[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirakawa19934–7-143) Buddhist and Jain texts sometimes have the same or similar titles but present different doctrines.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQvarnström2003ix–xi,_151–162-144)

Kings [Bimbisara](/wiki/Bimbisara "Bimbisara") (c. 558–491 BCE), [Ajatashatru](/wiki/Ajatashatru "Ajatashatru") (c. 492–460 BCE), and [Udayin](/wiki/Udayin "Udayin") (c. 460–440 BCE) of the [Haryanka dynasty](/wiki/Haryanka_dynasty "Haryanka dynasty") were patrons of Jainism.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp192541-145) Jain tradition states that [Chandragupta Maurya](/wiki/Chandragupta_Maurya "Chandragupta Maurya") (322–298 BCE), the founder of the [Mauryan Empire](/wiki/Mauryan_Empire "Mauryan Empire") and grandfather of [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka"), became a monk and disciple of Jain ascetic [Bhadrabahu](/wiki/Bhadrabahu "Bhadrabahu") in the later part of his life.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKulkeRothermund200463–65-146)[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoesche20037–18-147) Jain texts state that he died intentionally at Shravanabelagola by fasting.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKulkeRothermund200463–65-146)[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMookerji198839–46,_234–236-148)

### Epigraphic and archaeological evidence

The historicity of Jainism is supported by epigraphic and archaeological evidence.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUpinder_Singh2024273-149)[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaychaudhuri198813-150) The [Hathigumpha Inscription](/wiki/Hathigumpha_Inscription "Hathigumpha Inscription") at the [Udayagiri Caves](/wiki/Udayagiri_Caves "Udayagiri Caves") in Odisha, dated to the 2nd century BCE, is a key piece of early evidence.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUpinder_Singh2024273-149)[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaychaudhuri198813-150) This inscription, from King [Kharavela](/wiki/Kharavela "Kharavela") of [Kalinga](/wiki/Kalinga_(region) "Kalinga (region)"), details his patronage of Jain monks.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUpinder_Singh2024273-149)[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaychaudhuri198813-150) It also provides a historical reference by mentioning the retrieval of a Jina idol taken from Kalinga by a [Nanda dynasty](/wiki/Nanda_dynasty "Nanda dynasty") king (c. 4th century BCE).[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUpinder_Singh2024273-149)[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaychaudhuri198813-150)

Additionally, excavations at [Kankali Tila](/wiki/Kankali_Tila "Kankali Tila") in [Mathura](/wiki/Mathura "Mathura") have provided extensive archaeological evidence of an early Jain center.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort201030-151) The site yielded numerous Jain stupas, statues, and *ayagapatas* (votive tablets) dating from the 2nd century BCE to the 2nd century CE.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort201030-151) These tablets, commissioned by lay followers (*śrāvakas*), represent early physical evidence of an organized *sangha* (community) that included monks, nuns, and laity.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort201030-151)

The third century BCE emperor Ashoka, in his pillar edicts, mentions the *Niganthas* (Jains).[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp192543-152) *Tirthankara* statues date back to the second century BCE.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUpinder_Singh2024444-153) Archeological evidence suggests that Mathura was an important Jain center from the second century BCE. onwards.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJainFischer19789–10-154) Inscriptions from as early as the first century CE already show the schism between Digambara and Śvētāmbara.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp192549-155) There is inscriptional evidence for the presence of Jain monks in south India by the second or first centuries BCE, and archaeological evidence of Jain monks in Saurashtra in Gujarat by the second century CE.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2010202-156)
