### Sects and traditions

Main article: [Jain schools and branches](/wiki/Jain_schools_and_branches "Jain schools and branches")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Mahavir.jpg/120px-Mahavir.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mahavir.jpg)

Digambara [Mahāvīra](/wiki/Mah%C4%81v%C4%ABra "Mahāvīra") iconography

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Shri_Simandhar_Swami.jpg/120px-Shri_Simandhar_Swami.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shri_Simandhar_Swami.jpg)

Śvētāmbara [Simandhar Swami](/wiki/Simandhar_Swami "Simandhar Swami") iconography

The Jain community is divided into two major [denominations](/wiki/Religious_denomination "Religious denomination"), *[Digambara](/wiki/Digambara "Digambara")* and *[Śvētāmbara](/wiki/%C5%9Av%C4%93t%C4%81mbara "Śvētāmbara")*.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200245-157) This schism is ancient and centers on differing monastic disciplines, canonical scriptures, and core doctrines (such as the liberation of women).[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200245-157)

* The *Digambara* (sky-clad) tradition holds that male monks must renounce all possessions, including clothes, to practice *achailakya* (nudity).[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200245-157) Female monastics, known as *[Aryikas](/wiki/Aryika "Aryika")*, wear unstitched plain white [sarees](/wiki/Saree "Saree").[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200245-157)
* The *Śvētāmbara* (white-clad) tradition holds that monastics, both male and female, may wear simple, seamless white robes (*sachailakya*).[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200245-157)

#### The Great Schism: traditional accounts

The *Digambara* tradition places the origin of the schism around the 4th century BCE.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp192546-158) According to their account, *Acharya* [Bhadrabahu](/wiki/Bhadrabahu "Bhadrabahu") predicted a twelve-year famine in [Magadha](/wiki/Magadha "Magadha") and led a migration of monks to [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka").[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini19985-159)[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200247-160) [Sthulabhadra](/wiki/Sthulabhadra "Sthulabhadra"), a pupil, remained behind with other monks.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini19985-159)[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200247-160) The Digambara tradition holds that Sthulabhadra's northern group relaxed the original practice of nudity and began wearing white clothes, which was unacceptable to the returning monks who had preserved the *mūla sangha* (original community).[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini19985-159)[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200247-160) In this view, the *Digambaras* preserved the original *achailakya* (nude) practice of Mahavira, while the *Svetambaras* adopted a more lax, clothed practice.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini19985-159)[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200247-160)
The earliest record of Digambara beliefs is contained in the Prakrit *Suttapahuda* of [Kundakunda](/wiki/Kundakunda "Kundakunda").[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini19913-161)

The *Svetambara* tradition, in texts like the *Viśeṣāvaśyaka Bhāṣya* (5th cent. CE), places the schism much later.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Their account states the *Digambara* sect arose 609 years after Mahavira's *nirvana* (c. 1st-2nd cent. CE), founded by a monk named [Sivabhuti](/wiki/Sivabhuti "Sivabhuti").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The narrative states that Sivabhuti, in a "fit of pique," adopted nudity, which his original tradition rejected.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEB.S.L._Hanumantha_Rao1973-162)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurti2004-163)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBhandarkar1927-164)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHastingsSelbie1914704-165) Svetambara texts accuse this new sect of "eight concealments," including the rejection of the canonical texts preserved by their tradition and the new doctrine that women could not attain liberation.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200246–48-166)[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESogani1967-167)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaszkiewicz202424-168) The Śvētāmbara tradition in turn has two sub-traditions: Deravasi, also known as *[Mandirmargis](/w/index.php?title=Mandirmargi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mandirmargi (page does not exist)")*, and *Sthānakavasī*.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong201320–22-169)

#### Scholarly analysis and solidification

Most modern scholars, such as Padmanabh Jaini and Paul Dundas, conclude that the schism was not a single "event" but a gradual hardening of differences over several centuries.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200246–48-166) Monastic nudity and the wearing of robes likely co-existed as acceptable monastic options for some time.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200246–48-166) Archaeological evidence from Mathura, for instance, shows nude *tirthankara* images from the [Kushan Empire](/wiki/Kushan_Empire "Kushan Empire") (c. 1st century CE).[[165]](#cite_note-170)

The definitive, formal split is often associated with the [Council of Vallabhi](/wiki/Council_of_Vallabhi "Council of Vallabhi") in the 5th century CE.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice2010104–105-171)[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFohr201521–22-172) This council was organized by the *Svetambara* tradition to formally codify their canonical scriptures (the *Agamas*).[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice2010104–105-171)[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFohr201521–22-172) The *Digambara* tradition, which had its own (and different) scriptural canon, did not attend and rejected the authenticity of these texts, solidifying the schism.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice2010104–105-171)[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFohr201521–22-172)[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200246–48-166)

#### Key doctrinal and practical differences

Digambaras and Śvētāmbara differ in their practices and dress code,[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJonesRyan2007211-173)[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUmakant_P._Shah19875-174)[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200231–33-175) interpretations of teachings,[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUmakant_P._Shah19875-174)[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini200027–28-176) and on Jain history especially concerning the *tirthankaras*.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKailash_Chand_Jain199112-177)[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah200473–74-178)[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200221-179)[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUmakant_P._Shah198717-180)[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUmakant_P._Shah198779–80-181) Their monasticism rules differ,[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a167-182) as does their [iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography").[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a167-182) Śvētāmbara has had more female than male mendicants,[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a47-183) where Digambara has mostly had male monks[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlügel2006314–331,_353–361-184) and considers males closest to the soul's liberation.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong201336–37-185)[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarvey2016182–183-186) The Śvētāmbaras believe that women can also achieve liberation through asceticism[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarvey2016182–183-186)[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200255–59-187) and state that the 19th *Tirthankara* [Māllīnātha](/wiki/M%C4%81ll%C4%ABn%C4%81tha "Māllīnātha") was female,[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVallely200215-188) which Digambara rejects.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200256-189) Early Jain images from Mathura depict Digambara iconography until late fifth century CE where Svetambara iconography starts appearing.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVyas199516-190)

Several scholars and scriptures of other religions as well as those of their counterpart [Śvetāmbara](/wiki/%C5%9Avet%C4%81mbara "Śvetāmbara") Jains[[186]](#cite_note-191) [criticize Digambara sect's practices](/wiki/Digambara#Criticism_of_Digambara_sect "Digambara") of public nudity as well as their belief that women are incapable of attaining spiritual liberation.[[187]](#cite_note-192)[[188]](#cite_note-193)[[189]](#cite_note-194)

Excavations at Mathura revealed Jain statues from the time of the [Kushan Empire](/wiki/Kushan_Empire "Kushan Empire") (c. 1st century CE).[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini2000167-195) *Tirthankara* represented without clothes, and monks with cloth wrapped around the left arm, are identified as the *Ardhaphalaka* (half-clothed) mentioned in texts.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini2000167-195) The [Yapaniyas](/wiki/Yapaniya "Yapaniya"), believed to have originated from the *Ardhaphalaka*, followed Digambara nudity along with several Śvētāmbara beliefs.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini2000167-195) In the modern era, according to Flügel, new Jain religious movements that are a "primarily devotional form of Jainism" have developed which resemble "Jain Mahayana" style devotionalism.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlügel2005194–243-196)

### Medieval patronage and decline

Royal patronage has been a key factor in the growth and decline of Jainism.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah200469–70-197) In the second half of the first century CE, [Hindu kings](/wiki/Hindu_king "Hindu king") of the [Rashtrakuta](/wiki/Rashtrakuta "Rashtrakuta") dynasty sponsored major Jain cave temples.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPereira197721–24-198) King [Harshavardhana](/wiki/Harshavardhana "Harshavardhana") of the seventh century championed Jainism, Buddhism and all traditions of Hinduism.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp192552-199) The Pallava King [Mahendravarman I](/wiki/Mahendravarman_I "Mahendravarman I") (600–630 CE) converted from Jainism to Shaivism.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELochtefeld2002a409-200) His work *[Mattavilasa Prahasana](/wiki/Mattavilasa_Prahasana "Mattavilasa Prahasana")* ridicules certain Shaiva sects and the Buddhists and expresses contempt for Jain ascetics.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArunachalam1981170-201) The [Yadava dynasty](/wiki/Seuna_(Yadava)_dynasty "Seuna (Yadava) dynasty") built many temples at the [Ellora Caves](/wiki/Ellora_Caves "Ellora Caves") between 700 and 1000 CE.[[197]](#cite_note-202)[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGopal1990178-203)[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOwen20121–10-204) King [Āma](/wiki/%C4%80ma "Āma") of the eighth century converted to Jainism, and the Jain pilgrimage tradition was well established in his era.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp192552–54-205) [Mularaja](/wiki/Mularaja "Mularaja") (10th century CE), the founder of the [Chalukya dynasty](/wiki/Chalukya_dynasty "Chalukya dynasty"), constructed a Jain temple, even though he was not a Jain.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp192556-206) During the 11th century, [Basava](/wiki/Basava "Basava"), a minister to the Jain [Kalachuri](/wiki/Kalachuris_of_Kalyani "Kalachuris of Kalyani") king [Bijjala](/wiki/Bijjala_II "Bijjala II"), converted many Jains to the [Lingayat](/wiki/Lingayatism "Lingayatism") Shaivite sect. The Lingayats destroyed Jain temples and adapted them to their use.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp192575–77-207) The [Hoysala](/wiki/Hoysala_dynasty "Hoysala dynasty") King [Vishnuvardhana](/wiki/Vishnuvardhana "Vishnuvardhana") (c. 1108–1152 CE) became a [Vaishnavite](/wiki/Vaishnava "Vaishnava") under the influence of [Ramanuja](/wiki/Ramanuja "Ramanuja"), and Vaishnavism then grew rapidly in what is now Karnataka.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDas2005161-208)

[![Jain monuments in Nagarparkar, Pakistan](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Gori_Mandar.jpg/250px-Gori_Mandar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gori_Mandar.jpg)

The ruins of [Gori Jain temples](/wiki/Gori_Temple,_Nagarparkar "Gori Temple, Nagarparkar") in [Nagarparkar](/wiki/Nagarparkar "Nagarparkar"), Pakistan, a pilgrimage site before 1947[[204]](#cite_note-:0-209)

Jainism faced persecution during and after the [Muslim conquests on the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Muslim_conquests_on_the_Indian_subcontinent "Muslim conquests on the Indian subcontinent"). The scholarship in context of Jain relations with the ruler of [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") remains scarce, notwithstanding there were several instances of cordial relations of Jains with prominent rulers of the Sultanate. [Alauddin Khalji](/wiki/Alauddin_Khalji "Alauddin Khalji") (1296–1316), as attested by the Jain texts held discussions with Jain sages and once specially summoned Acharya Mahasena to Delhi.[[205]](#cite_note-210) One more prominent Jain figure Acharya Ramachandra Suri was also honored by him. During his reign, his governor of Gujarat, [Alp Khan](/wiki/Alp_Khan "Alp Khan") permitted the reconstruction of the temples razed during earlier Muslim conquests and himself made huge donation for the renovation of Jain temples.[[206]](#cite_note-Pushpa-211)[[207]](#cite_note-212) [Muhammad bin Tughluq](/wiki/Muhammad_bin_Tughluq "Muhammad bin Tughluq") (1325–1351) according to the Jain chronicles favoured the Jain scholars.[[208]](#cite_note-213)

The [Mughal emperors](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") in general were influenced by the Jain scholars and made patronage and grants for their pilgrimage sites under [Humayun](/wiki/Humayun "Humayun") (1540–1556), [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar") (1556–1605), [Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir") (1605–1627) and even [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb") (1658–1707).[[209]](#cite_note-214) Despite this, there were instances of religious bigotry during the Mughal rule towards Jains. [Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur") (1526–1530), the first Mughal emperor ordered the destruction of various Jain idols in [Gwalior](/wiki/Gwalior "Gwalior").[[210]](#cite_note-215) In 1567, Akbar [ravaged the fort of Chittor](/wiki/Siege_of_Chittorgarh_(1567-1568) "Siege of Chittorgarh (1567-1568)"). After the conquest of the fort, Akbar ordered the destruction of several Jain shrines and temples in Chittor.[[211]](#cite_note-216) Similarly there were instances of desecration of Jain religious shrines under [Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir"), [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan") and most notably under [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb").[[212]](#cite_note-217)

The Jain community were the traditional bankers and financiers, and this significantly impacted the Muslim rulers. However, they rarely were a part of the political power during the Islamic rule period of the Indian subcontinent.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort199885–86-218)

### Colonial era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Virchand_Gandhi_poster.jpg/250px-Virchand_Gandhi_poster.jpg)](/wiki/File:Virchand_Gandhi_poster.jpg)

A poster of [Virchand Gandhi](/wiki/Virchand_Gandhi "Virchand Gandhi"), who represented Jainism at the [Parliament of the World's Religions](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_World%27s_Religions "Parliament of the World's Religions") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") in 1893

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Shrimad_Rajchandra_Spiritual_Centre_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Shrimad_Rajchandra_Spiritual_Centre_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shrimad_Rajchandra_Spiritual_Centre_(cropped).jpg)

A 34-foot-tall idol of [Shrimad Rajchandra](/wiki/Shrimad_Rajchandra "Shrimad Rajchandra") at [Dharampur](/wiki/Dharampur,_Gujarat "Dharampur, Gujarat"), [Valsad](/wiki/Valsad_district "Valsad district")

A Gujarati Jain scholar, [Virchand Gandhi](/wiki/Virchand_Gandhi "Virchand Gandhi"), represented Jainism at the first [World Parliament of Religions](/wiki/World_Parliament_of_Religions "World Parliament of Religions") in 1893, held in America during the [Chicago World's Fair](/wiki/World%27s_Columbian_Exposition "World's Columbian Exposition"). He worked to defend the rights of Jains and wrote and lectured extensively on Jainism.[[214]](#cite_note-it-219)[[215]](#cite_note-OpenCourt-220)

[Shrimad Rajchandra](/wiki/Shrimad_Rajchandra "Shrimad Rajchandra"), a mystic, poet and philosopher from [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") is believed to have attained *jatismaran gnana* (ability to recollect past lives) at the age of seven. Virchand Gandhi mentioned this feat at the Parliament of the World's Religions.[[216]](#cite_note-KarbhariGāndhī1911-221) He is best known because of his association with Mahatma Gandhi.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalter2002145-222) Shrimad Rajchandra composed [Shri Atmasiddhi Shastra](/wiki/Atma_Siddhi "Atma Siddhi"), considered his magnum opus, containing the essence of Jainism in a single sitting of 1.5–2 hours.[[218]](#cite_note-223) He expounds on the six fundamental truths of the soul:[[219]](#cite_note-224)

1. Self (soul) exists
2. It is permanent and eternal
3. It is the doer of its own actions
4. It is the enjoyer or the sufferer of its actions
5. Liberation exists
6. There is a path to achieve liberation.

Colonial era reports and Christian missions variously viewed Jainism as a sect of Hinduism, a sect of Buddhism, or a distinct religion.[[220]](#cite_note-225)[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHopkins1902283-226)[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESunavala193491–93-227) Christian missionaries were frustrated at Jain people without pagan creator gods refusing to convert to Christianity, while colonial era Jain scholars such as [Champat Rai Jain](/wiki/Champat_Rai_Jain "Champat Rai Jain") defended Jainism against criticism and misrepresentation by Christian activists.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini200033-228) Missionaries of Christianity and Islam considered Jain traditions idolatrous and superstitious.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHackett200863–68-229) These criticisms, states [John E. Cort](/wiki/John_E._Cort "John E. Cort"), were flawed and ignored similar practices within sects of Christianity.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort201012–16,_200–207,_218–219,_251_with_note_10-230)

The British colonial government in India and Indian princely states promoted religious tolerance. However, laws were passed that made roaming naked by anyone an arrestable crime. This drew popular support from the majority Hindu population, but particularly impacted Digambara monks.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlügel2006348–349-231) The Akhil Bharatiya Jain Samaj opposed this law, claiming that it interfered with Jain religious rights. Acharya [Shantisagar](/wiki/Shantisagar "Shantisagar") entered Bombay (now Mumbai) in 1927, but was forced to cover his body. He then led an India-wide tour as the naked monk with his followers, to various Digambara sacred sites, and was welcomed by kings of the Maharashtra provinces.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlügel2006348–349-231) Shantisagar fasted to oppose the restrictions imposed on Digambara monks by the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") and prompted their discontinuance.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah200456-232) The laws were abolished by India after independence.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlügel2006359–360-233)

### Modern era

The texts attributed to [Kundakunda](/wiki/Kundakunda "Kundakunda") inspired two contemporary lay-movements within Jainism with his notion of [two truths](/wiki/Two_truths_doctrine "Two truths doctrine") and his emphasis on direct insight into *niścayanaya* or 'ultimate perspective', also called "supreme" ([paramārtha](/wiki/Param%C4%81rtha "Paramārtha")) and "pure" (śuddha).[[f]](#cite_note-237)

[Shrimad Rajchandra](/wiki/Shrimad_Rajchandra "Shrimad Rajchandra") (1867-1901) was a Jain poet and mystic who was inspired by works of [Kundakunda](/wiki/Kundakunda "Kundakunda") and [Digambara](/wiki/Digambara "Digambara") mystical tradition. Nominally belonging to the Digambara tradition,[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrakash_C._Jain20257,9-238) his followers sometimes consider his teaching as a new path of Jainism, neither [Śvetāmbara](/wiki/%C5%9Avet%C4%81mbara "Śvetāmbara") nor [Digambara](/wiki/Digambara "Digambara"), and revere him as a saint. His path is sometimes referred as *Raj Bhakta Marg*, *Kavipanth*, or *Shrimadiya*, which has mostly lay followers as was Rajchandra himself.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalter2002-239) His teachings influenced [Kanji Swami](/wiki/Kanji_Swami "Kanji Swami"), [Dada Bhagwan](/wiki/Dada_Bhagwan "Dada Bhagwan"),[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlügel2005194–243-196) [Rakesh Jhaveri](/wiki/Rakesh_Jhaveri "Rakesh Jhaveri") (Shrimad Rajchandra Mission), [Saubhagbhai](/wiki/Saubhagyabhai "Saubhagyabhai"), [Lalluji Maharaj](/wiki/Lalluji_Muni "Lalluji Muni") (Laghuraj Swami), Atmanandji and several other religious figures.

Kanji Panth is a lay movement founded by [Kanji Swami](/wiki/Kanji_Swami "Kanji Swami") (1890-1980).[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw,_Richard-240) Nominally it belongs to the [Śvetāmbara](/wiki/%C5%9Avet%C4%81mbara "Śvetāmbara")[[235]](#cite_note-philtar_Jainism-241) but is inspired by Kundakunda and [Shrimad Rajchandra](/wiki/Shrimad_Rajchandra "Shrimad Rajchandra") (1867-1901), though "lacking a place in any Digambara ascetic lineage descending from Kundakunda."[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw,_Richard-240) Kanji Swami has many followers in the Jain diaspora.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer2004464-242) They generally regard themselves simply as Digambara Jains, more popularly known as *Mumukshu*, following the mystical tradition of Kundakunda and Pandit Todarmal.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw,_Richard-240)

Bauer notes that "[in] recent years there has been a convergence of the Kanji Swami Panth and the Shrimad Rajcandra movement, part of trend toward a more eucumenical and less sectarian Jainism among educated, mobile Jains living overseas."[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer2004465-243)

The [Akram Vignan Movement](/wiki/Akram_Vignan_Movement "Akram Vignan Movement") established by [Dada Bhagwan](/wiki/Dada_Bhagwan "Dada Bhagwan") draws inspiration from teachings of Rajchandra and other Jain scriptures, though it is considered as a Jain-Vaishnava Hindu [syncretistic](/wiki/Religious_syncretism "Religious syncretism") movement.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlügel2005-244)

Greatly influenced by [Shrimad Rajchandra](/wiki/Shrimad_Rajchandra "Shrimad Rajchandra"), the leader of the campaign for Indian independence, [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi") stated regarding Jainism:[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERudolphRudolph1984171-245)

> No religion in the World has explained the principle of *Ahiṃsā* so deeply and systematically as is discussed with its applicability in every human life in Jainism. As and when the benevolent principle of *Ahiṃsā* or non-violence will be ascribed for practice by the people of the world to achieve their end of life in this world and beyond, Jainism is sure to have the uppermost status and Mahāvīra is sure to be respected as the greatest authority on *Ahiṃsā*.[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJanardan_Pandey199850-246)

[Chandanaji](/wiki/Acharya_Chandana "Acharya Chandana") became the first Jain woman to receive the title of [Acharya](/wiki/Acharya "Acharya") in 1987.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerLongReading2019[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid_9i_DwAAQBAJpgPA10_7,_10–]-247)

## Practices

### The Four-Fold Community (*Caturvidha Saṅgha*)

Main article: [Jain monasticism](/wiki/Jain_monasticism "Jain monasticism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Ellora%2C_cave_33%2C_Digambar_Jain_guru_%289841591645%29.jpg/120px-Ellora%2C_cave_33%2C_Digambar_Jain_guru_%289841591645%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ellora,_cave_33,_Digambar_Jain_guru_(9841591645).jpg)

Digambara sadhu (monk)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Acharya_Vijayavallabhasuri.jpg/120px-Acharya_Vijayavallabhasuri.jpg)](/wiki/File:Acharya_Vijayavallabhasuri.jpg)

Śvētāmbara-Deravasi sadhu (monk)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Jain_Sthanakvasi_monk.jpg/120px-Jain_Sthanakvasi_monk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jain_Sthanakvasi_monk.jpg)

Śvētāmbara-Sthanakwasi sadhu (monk)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Jain_1.jpg/120px-Jain_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jain_1.jpg)

A Śvētāmbara sadhvi (nun)(early 20th-century)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Viramati_Mataji.jpg/250px-Viramati_Mataji.jpg)](/wiki/File:Viramati_Mataji.jpg)

A Digambara sadhvi (nun)

Of the major Indian religions, Jainism has had the strongest ascetic tradition.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a118–122-248)[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQvarnström2003113-249)[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQvarnström2003169–174,_178–198_with_footnotes-250) Ascetic life may include nakedness, symbolizing non-possession even of clothes, fasting, body mortification, and penance, to burn away past karma and stop producing new karma, both of which are believed essential for reaching *siddha* and *moksha* ("liberation from rebirths" and "salvation").[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a118–122-248)[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQvarnström2003205–212_with_footnotes-251)[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBalcerowicz2015144–150-252)

Jain texts like *Tattvartha Sūtra* and *[Uttaradhyayana Sūtra](/wiki/Uttaradhyayana "Uttaradhyayana")* discuss austerities in detail. Six outer and six inner practices are oft-repeated in later Jain texts.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a120–21-253) Outer austerities include complete fasting, eating limited amounts, eating restricted items, abstaining from tasty foods, mortifying the flesh, and guarding the flesh (avoiding anything that is a source of temptation).[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a120–122-254) Inner austerities include expiation, confession, respecting and assisting [mendicants](/wiki/Mendicants "Mendicants"), studying, meditation, and ignoring bodily wants in order to abandon the body.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a120–122-254) Lists of internal and external austerities vary with the text and tradition.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQvarnström2003182_with_footnote_3-255)[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson1995196–197-256) Asceticism is viewed as a means to control desires, and to purify the *jiva* (soul).[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQvarnström2003169–174,_178–198_with_footnotes-250) According to tradition, the *tirthankaras* such as the Mahāvīra (Vardhamana) set an example by performing severe austerities for twelve years.[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a121–122-257)[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShanti_Lal_Jain199851-258)[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBalcerowicz201515–18,_41–43-259)

Monastic organization, *sangh*, has a four-fold order consisting of *sadhu* (male ascetics, *muni*), *sadhvi* (female ascetics, *aryika*), *[śrāvaka](/wiki/%C5%9Ar%C4%81vaka_(Jainism) "Śrāvaka (Jainism)")* (laymen), and *śrāvikā* (laywomen).[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002150-260) The latter two support the ascetics and their monastic organizations called *gacch* or *samuday*, in autonomous regional Jain congregations.[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a48–49-261)[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBalcerowicz200917-262)[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah20042–3-263) Jain monastic rules have encouraged the use of mouth cover, as well as the *Dandasan* – a long stick with woolen threads – to gently remove ants and insects that may come in their path.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVijay_K._Jain2013197-264)[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002152,_163–164-265)[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998190-266)

In Jainism, six essential duties (*avashyakas*) are prescribed for *[śrāvakas](/wiki/%C5%9Ar%C4%81vaka_(Jainism) "Śrāvaka (Jainism)")* (householders).[[261]](#cite_note-psja-267)[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah1998171–173-268) The six duties are:[[263]](#cite_note-269)

1. Worship of *[Pañca-Parameṣṭhi](/wiki/Pa%C3%B1ca-Parame%E1%B9%A3%E1%B9%ADhi "Pañca-Parameṣṭhi")* (five supreme beings)
2. Following the preachings of Jain saints.
3. Study of [Jain scriptures](/wiki/Jain_scriptures "Jain scriptures")
4. *[Samayika](/wiki/Samayika "Samayika")*: practising serenity and meditation
5. Following discipline in their daily engagement
6. Charity (*dāna*) of four kinds:[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERam_Bhushan_Prasad_Singh200882–83Sangave2001[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidQzEQJHWUwXQCpgPA157_157]-270)
   1. *Ahara-dāna*- donation of food
   2. *Ausadha-dāna*- donation of medicine
   3. *Jnana-dāna*- donation of knowledge
   4. *Abhaya-dāna*- saving the life of a living being or giving of protection to someone under threat

These duties became fundamental ritual activities of a [Jain](/wiki/Jain "Jain") householder. Such as spreading the [grain](/wiki/Grain "Grain") for the [birds](/wiki/Birds "Birds") in the morning, and [filtering](/wiki/Filter_(water) "Filter (water)") or [boiling](/wiki/Boiling "Boiling") the water for the next few hours' use became ritual acts of [charity](/wiki/Charity_(virtue) "Charity (virtue)") and [non-violence](/wiki/Non-violence "Non-violence").[[261]](#cite_note-psja-267) *Samayika* was used as a word for all spiritual activity including icon worship during medieval times.[[261]](#cite_note-psja-267)

### *Ahimsa* in practice: Jain diet

Main articles: [Jain vegetarianism](/wiki/Jain_vegetarianism "Jain vegetarianism") and [Fasting in Jainism](/wiki/Fasting_in_Jainism "Fasting in Jainism")

The practice of non-violence towards all living beings has led to Jain culture being [vegetarian](/wiki/Jain_vegetarianism "Jain vegetarianism"). Devout Jains practice [lacto-vegetarianism](/wiki/Lacto-vegetarianism "Lacto-vegetarianism"), meaning that they eat no eggs, but accept dairy products if there is no violence against animals during their production. [Veganism](/wiki/Veganism "Veganism") is encouraged if there are concerns about animal welfare.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVoorst2015105-271) Jain monks, nuns and some followers avoid [root vegetables](/wiki/Root_vegetable "Root vegetable") such as potatoes, onions, and garlic because tiny organisms are injured when the plant is pulled up, and because a bulb or tuber's ability to sprout is seen as characteristic of a higher living being.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESangave1980260-272)[[g]](#cite_note-273) Jain monks and advanced lay people avoid eating after sunset, observing a vow of *ratri-bhojana-tyaga-vrata*.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini2000285-274) Monks observe a stricter vow by eating only once a day.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini2000285-274)

Jains fast particularly during festivals.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200985-275) This practice is called *upavasa*, *tapasya* or *vrata*,[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200985–86-276) and may be practiced according to one's ability.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERam_Bhushan_Prasad_Singh200892–94-277) Digambaras fast for *Dasa-laksana-parvan*, eating only one or two meals per day, drinking only boiled water for ten days, or fasting completely on the first and last days of the festival,[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200972-278) mimicking the practices of a Jain mendicant for the period.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200972-278) Śvētāmbara Jains do similarly in the eight day *paryusana* with *samvatsari-pratikramana*.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200972,_85–86-279) The practice is believed to remove karma from one's soul and provides merit (*punya*).[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200985-275) A "one day" fast lasts about 36 hours, starting at sunset before the day of the fast and ending 48 minutes after sunrise the day after.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200985-275) Among laypeople, fasting is more commonly observed by women, as it shows their piety and religious purity, gains merit earning and helps ensure future well-being for their family. Some religious fasts are observed in a social and supportive female group.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200986-280) Long fasts are celebrated by friends and families with special ceremonies.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200986-280)

### Meditation - *sāmāyika*

Main article: [Jain meditation](/wiki/Jain_meditation "Jain meditation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Jain_meditation.jpg/250px-Jain_meditation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jain_meditation.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Shravanabelagola_Bahubali_wideframe.jpg/250px-Shravanabelagola_Bahubali_wideframe.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shravanabelagola_Bahubali_wideframe.jpg)

Left: Jain nuns meditating, Right: 10th-century [Gommateshwara statue](/wiki/Gommateshwara_statue "Gommateshwara statue") depicting standing meditation ([Kayotsarga](/wiki/Kayotsarga "Kayotsarga") posture)

Jainism considers meditation (*dhyana*) a necessary practice, but it never has been a central practice.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002166–169-281) In Jainism, meditation is concerned more with stopping karmic attachments and activity, not as a means to transformational insights or self-realization in other Indian religions.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002166–169-281)

According to [Padmanabh Jaini](/wiki/Padmanabh_Jaini "Padmanabh Jaini"), *[Sāmāyika](/wiki/S%C4%81m%C4%81yika "Sāmāyika")* is a practice of "brief periods in meditation" in Jainism that is a part of *siksavrata* (ritual restraint).[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998180–181-282) The goal of *Sāmāyika* is to achieve equanimity, and it is the second *siksavrata*.[[h]](#cite_note-283) The *samayika* ritual is practiced at least three times a day by mendicants, while a layperson includes it with other ritual practices such as *Puja* in a Jain temple and doing charity work.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998180–182-284)[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTES.A._Jain1992261-285)[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah2004128–131-286) According to Johnson, as well as Jaini, *samayika* connotes more than meditation, and for a Jain householder is the voluntary ritual practice of "assuming temporary ascetic status".[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson1995189–190-287)[[i]](#cite_note-289)

### Devotion, worship, and prayer

Main article: [Jain rituals](/wiki/Jain_rituals "Jain rituals")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Shravanbelgola_Gomateshvara_feet_prayer1.jpg/250px-Shravanbelgola_Gomateshvara_feet_prayer1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shravanbelgola_Gomateshvara_feet_prayer1.jpg)

Praying at the feet of a statue of [Bahubali](/wiki/Bahubali "Bahubali")

There are many rituals in Jainism's various sects. According to Dundas, the ritualistic lay path among Śvētāmbara Jains is "heavily imbued with ascetic values", where the rituals either revere or celebrate the ascetic life of tirthankaras, or progressively approach the psychological and physical life of an ascetic.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002187–189-290)[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998162–165,_295–296-291) The ultimate ritual is *sallekhana*, a religious death through ascetic abandonment of food and drinks.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002187–189-290) The Digambara Jains follow the same theme, but the life cycle and religious rituals are closer to a Hindu liturgy.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002187–189-290) The overlap is mainly in the life cycle (rites-of-passage) rituals, and likely developed because Jain and Hindu societies overlapped, and rituals were viewed as necessary and secular.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998291–299-292)[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley2009186–187-293)

Jains ritually worship numerous deities,[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998162–165,_295–296-291) especially the *[Jinas](/wiki/Arihant_(Jainism) "Arihant (Jainism)")*. In Jainism a *Jina* as *deva* is not an [avatar](/wiki/Avatar "Avatar") (incarnation), but the highest state of omniscience that an ascetic *tirthankara* achieved.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998295–299-294) Out of the 24 tirthankaras, Jains predominantly worship four: Mahāvīra, [Parshvanatha](/wiki/Parshvanatha "Parshvanatha"), [Neminatha](/wiki/Neminatha "Neminatha") and [Rishabhanatha](/wiki/Rishabhanatha "Rishabhanatha").[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200240-295) Among the non-*tirthankara* saints, devotional worship is common for [Bahubali](/wiki/Bahubali "Bahubali") among the Digambaras.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2010182–184-296) The *[Panch Kalyanaka](/wiki/Panch_Kalyanaka "Panch Kalyanaka")* rituals remember the five life events of the *tirthankaras*, including the *[Panch Kalyanaka Pratishtha Mahotsava](/wiki/Panch_Kalyanaka_Pratishtha_Mahotsava "Panch Kalyanaka Pratishtha Mahotsava")*, *Panch Kalyanaka Puja* and *Snatrapuja*.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998196,_343,_347-297)[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998196–199-298)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/A_Jain_ritual_offerings_and_puja_recital_at_a_temple%2C_worship_in_Jainism.jpg/250px-A_Jain_ritual_offerings_and_puja_recital_at_a_temple%2C_worship_in_Jainism.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Jain_ritual_offerings_and_puja_recital_at_a_temple,_worship_in_Jainism.jpg)

Jain worship may include ritual offerings and recitals.[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200945–46,_215-299)

The basic ritual is *darsana* (seeing) of *deva*, which includes Jina,[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELindsay_Jones20054771-300) or other [yaksas](/wiki/Yaksha "Yaksha"), gods and goddesses such as Brahmadeva, 52 Viras, [Padmavati](/wiki/Padmavati_(Jainism) "Padmavati (Jainism)"), [Ambika](/wiki/Ambika_(Jainism) "Ambika (Jainism)") and 16 Vidyadevis (including [Sarasvati](/wiki/Sarasvati "Sarasvati") and [Lakshmi](/wiki/Lakshmi "Lakshmi")).[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200933,_59,_92,_138,_191-301)[[293]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort1987235–255-302)[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMishraRay2016141–148-303) Terapanthi Digambaras limit their ritual worship to tirthankaras.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a365-304) The worship ritual is called *devapuja*, and is found in all Jain sub-traditions.[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998199–200-305) Typically, the Jain layperson enters the Derasar (Jain temple) inner sanctum in simple clothing and bare feet with a plate filled with offerings, bows down, says the [namaskar](/wiki/Namaskar "Namaskar"), completes his or her litany and prayers, sometimes is assisted by the temple priest, leaves the offerings and then departs.[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998199–200-305)

Jain practices include performing *[abhisheka](/wiki/Abhisheka "Abhisheka")* (ceremonial bath) of the images.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPratapaditya_Pal198629-306) Some Jain sects employ a *pujari* (also called *upadhye*), who may be a Hindu, to perform priestly duties at the temple.[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002204–205-307)[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalvadori1989169–170-308) More elaborate worship includes offerings such as rice, fresh and dry fruits, flowers, coconut, sweets, and money. Some may light up a lamp with camphor and make auspicious marks with sandalwood paste. Devotees also recite Jain texts, particularly the life stories of the tirthankaras.[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBabb199632–33-309)[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200945–46,_215-299)

Traditional Jains, like Buddhists and Hindus, believe in the efficacy of [mantras](/wiki/Mantra "Mantra") and that certain sounds and words are inherently auspicious, powerful and spiritual.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200281–82-310)[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENayanar200535-311) The most famous of the mantras, broadly accepted in various sects of Jainism, is the "five homage" (*[panca namaskara](/wiki/Namokar_Mantra "Namokar Mantra")*) mantra which is believed to be eternal and existent since the first tirthankara's time.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200281–82-310)[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVoorst2015107-312) Medieval worship practices included making tantric diagrams of the *Rishi-mandala* including the *tirthankaras*.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGough20121–47-313) The Jain tantric traditions use mantra and rituals that are believed to accrue merit for rebirth realms.[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001b417–419-314)

### Festivals

Main article: [Jain festivals](/wiki/Jain_festivals "Jain festivals")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Das_Lakshana_%28Paryusana%29_celebrations%2C_New_York_City_Jain_temple_2.JPG/250px-Das_Lakshana_%28Paryusana%29_celebrations%2C_New_York_City_Jain_temple_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Das_Lakshana_(Paryusana)_celebrations,_New_York_City_Jain_temple_2.JPG)

Celebrating Das Lakshana (Paryushana), [Jain Center of America](/wiki/Jain_Center_of_America "Jain Center of America"), New York City

The most important annual Jain festival is called the *[Paryushana](/wiki/Paryushana "Paryushana")* by Svetambaras and *[Dasa lakshana parva](/w/index.php?title=Dasha-Lakshana-Parvan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dasha-Lakshana-Parvan (page does not exist)")* by the Digambaras. It is celebrated from the 12th day of the waning moon in the traditional lunisolar month of [Bhadrapada](/wiki/Bhadra_(Hindu_calendar) "Bhadra (Hindu calendar)") in the [Indian calendar](/wiki/Hindu_calendar "Hindu calendar"). This typically falls in August or September of the Gregorian calendar.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a164,_284-315)[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelton2011673-316) It lasts eight days for Svetambaras, and ten days among the Digambaras.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a164,_284-315) It is a time when lay people fast and pray. The five vows are emphasized during this time.[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelton2011673-316) Svetambaras recite the *Kalpasūtras*, while Digambaras read their own texts. The festival is an occasion where Jains make active effort to stop cruelty towards other life forms, freeing animals in captivity and preventing the slaughter of animals.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a164,_284-315)

> **Forgiveness**
>
> I forgive all living beings,  
> may all living beings forgive me.  
> All in this world are my friends,  
> I have no enemies.

— *Jain festival prayer on the last day*[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a284-317)

The last day involves a focused prayer and meditation session known as *[Samvatsari](/wiki/Samvatsari "Samvatsari")*. Jains consider this a day of atonement, granting forgiveness to others, seeking forgiveness from all living beings, physically or mentally asking for forgiveness and resolving to treat everyone in the world as friends.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a164,_284-315) Forgiveness is asked by saying "*[Micchami Dukkadam](/wiki/Micchami_Dukkadam "Micchami Dukkadam")*" or "*Khamat khamna*" to others. This means, "If I have offended you in any way, knowingly or unknowingly, in thought, word or action, then I seek your forgiveness." The literal meaning of *Paryushana* is "abiding" or "coming together".[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort1995160-318)

[Mahavir Janma Kalyanak](/wiki/Mahavir_Jayanti "Mahavir Jayanti") celebrates the birth of Mahāvīra. It is celebrated on the 13th day of the lunisolar month of [Chaitra](/wiki/Chaitra "Chaitra") in the traditional Indian calendar. This typically falls in March or April of the Gregorian calendar.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a220-319)[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah2004211-320) The festivities include visiting Jain temples, [pilgrimages](/wiki/Tirtha_(Jainism) "Tirtha (Jainism)") to shrines, reading Jain texts and processions of Mahāvīra by the community. At his legendary birthplace of Kundagrama in [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"), north of Patna, special events are held by Jains.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a220-319) [The next day of Dipawali](/wiki/Diwali_(Jainism) "Diwali (Jainism)") is observed by Jains as the anniversary of Mahāvīra's attainment of *[moksha](/wiki/Moksha_(Jainism) "Moksha (Jainism)")*.[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPechilisRaj201386-321) The Hindu festival of [Diwali](/wiki/Diwali "Diwali") is also celebrated on the same date (*Kartika Amavasya*). Jain temples, homes, offices, and shops are decorated with lights and [*diyas*](/wiki/Diya_(lamp) "Diya (lamp)") (small oil lamps). The lights are symbolic of knowledge or removal of ignorance. Sweets are often distributed. On Diwali morning, *Nirvan Ladoo* is offered after praying to Mahāvīra in all Jain temples across the world. The Jain new year starts right after Diwali.[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPechilisRaj201386-321) Some other festivals celebrated by Jains are [Akshaya Tritiya](/wiki/Akshaya_Tritiya "Akshaya Tritiya") and [Raksha Bandhan](/wiki/Raksha_Bandhan "Raksha Bandhan"), similar to those in the Hindu communities.[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPechilisRaj201385-322)[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a164-323)

## Scriptures and texts

Main article: [Jain literature](/wiki/Jain_literature "Jain literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Jain_Agamas.jpeg/120px-Jain_Agamas.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Jain_Agamas.jpeg)

Stela depicting *Śhrut Jnāna*, or complete scriptural knowledge

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Suryaprajnapati_Sutra.jpg/250px-Suryaprajnapati_Sutra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Suryaprajnapati_Sutra.jpg)

The Suryaprajnaptisūtra, a fourth or third century BCE astronomy text of Śvētāmbara Jains. Above: its manuscript from c. 1500 CE.[[315]](#cite_note-324)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Mangulam_inscription.jpg/250px-Mangulam_inscription.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mangulam_inscription.jpg)

[Mangulam](/wiki/Mangulam "Mangulam") inscription dated 3rd century BCE

Jain canonical scriptures are called *Agamas*. They are believed to have been [verbally transmitted](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition"), much like the ancient Buddhist and Hindu texts,[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200260–61-325) and to have originated from the sermons of the *tirthankaras*, whereupon the *[Ganadharas](/wiki/Ganadhara "Ganadhara")* (chief disciples) transmitted them as *Śhrut Jnāna* (heard knowledge).[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChampat_Rai_Jain1929b135–136-326)[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925109–110-327) The spoken scriptural language is believed to be *[Ardhamagadhi](/wiki/Ardhamagadhi "Ardhamagadhi")* by the Śvētāmbara Jains, and a form of sonic resonance by the Digambara Jains.[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200260–61-325)

The Śvētāmbaras believe that they have preserved 45 of the 50 original Jain scriptures (having lost an Anga text and four Purva texts), while the Digambaras believe that all were lost,[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200261-328)[[320]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925112–113,_121–122-329) and that Āchārya Bhutabali was the last ascetic who had partial knowledge of the original canon. According to them, Digambara *Āchāryas* recreated the oldest-known Digambara Jain texts, including the four *anuyoga*.[[321]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVijay_K._Jain2016xii-330)[[322]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini199878–81-331)[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925124-332) The Digambara texts partially agree with older Śvētāmbara texts, but there are also gross differences between the texts of the two major Jain traditions.[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925121–122-333) The Digambaras created a secondary canon between 600 and 900 CE, compiling it into four groups or *Vedas*: history, cosmography, philosophy and ethics.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925123–124-334)[[j]](#cite_note-336)

The most popular and influential texts of Jainism have been its non-canonical literature. Of these, the *[Kalpa Sūtras](/wiki/Kalpa_S%C5%ABtra "Kalpa Sūtra")* are particularly popular among Śvētāmbaras, which they attribute to Bhadrabahu (c. 300 BCE). This ancient scholar is revered in the Digambara tradition, and they believe he led their migration into the ancient south Karnataka region and created their tradition.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925125–126-337) Śvētāmbaras believe instead that Bhadrabahu moved to Nepal.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925125–126-337) Both traditions consider his *Niryuktis* and *Samhitas* important. The earliest surviving Sanskrit text by [Umaswati](/wiki/Umaswati "Umaswati"), the *[Tattvarthasūtra](/wiki/Tattvarthas%C5%ABtra "Tattvarthasūtra")* is considered authoritative by all traditions of Jainism.[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJonesRyan2007439–440-338)[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2006395–396-339)[[k]](#cite_note-341) In the Digambara tradition, the texts attributed to [Kundakunda](/wiki/Kundakunda "Kundakunda") are highly revered and have been historically influential,[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFinegan1989221-342)[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBalcerowicz200325–34-343)[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChatterjee2000282–283-344) while the oldest being *[Kasayapahuda](/wiki/Kasayapahuda "Kasayapahuda")* and *[Shatkhandagama](/wiki/Shatkhandagama "Shatkhandagama")* attributed to Acharya pushpdanta and Bhutbali. Other important Digambara Jain texts include: *[Samayasara](/wiki/Samayasara "Samayasara")*, *[Ratnakaranda śrāvakācāra](/wiki/Ratnakaranda_%C5%9Br%C4%81vak%C4%81c%C4%81ra "Ratnakaranda śrāvakācāra")*, and *[Niyamasara](/wiki/Niyamasara "Niyamasara")*.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini199132–33-345)

The Digambara-texts attributed to [Kundakunda](/wiki/Kundakunda "Kundakunda") inspired [Shrimad Rajchandra](/wiki/Shrimad_Rajchandra "Shrimad Rajchandra") (1867-1901), who in turn influenced [Kanji Swami](/wiki/Kanji_Swami "Kanji Swami") (Kanji Path), [Dada Bhagwan](/wiki/Dada_Bhagwan "Dada Bhagwan"),[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlügel2005194–243-196) [Rakesh Jhaveri](/wiki/Rakesh_Jhaveri "Rakesh Jhaveri") (Shrimad Rajchandra Mission), [Saubhagbhai](/wiki/Saubhagyabhai "Saubhagyabhai"), [Lalluji Maharaj](/wiki/Lalluji_Muni "Lalluji Muni") (Laghuraj Swami), Atmanandji and several other religious figures. According to Bauer, "[in] recent years there has been a convergence of the Kanji Swami Panth and the Shrimad Rajcandra movement, part of trend toward a more eucumenical and less sectarian Jainism among educated, mobile Jains living overseas."[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer2004465-243)

## Culture (art, architecture)

Main article: [Jain art](/wiki/Jain_art "Jain art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Detail_of_a_leaf_with_the_birth_of_mahavira.jpg/250px-Detail_of_a_leaf_with_the_birth_of_mahavira.jpg)](/wiki/File:Detail_of_a_leaf_with_the_birth_of_mahavira.jpg)

The birth of Mahavira, from the *[Kalpa Sūtra](/wiki/Kalpa_S%C5%ABtra "Kalpa Sūtra")* (c. 1375–1400 CE)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/%22Sihanamdika_ayagapata%22%2C_Jain_votive_plate%2C_Kankali_Tila%2C_Mathura_dated_25-50_CE.jpg/250px-%22Sihanamdika_ayagapata%22%2C_Jain_votive_plate%2C_Kankali_Tila%2C_Mathura_dated_25-50_CE.jpg)](/wiki/File:%22Sihanamdika_ayagapata%22,_Jain_votive_plate,_Kankali_Tila,_Mathura_dated_25-50_CE.jpg)

[Sihanamdika ayagapata](/wiki/Ayagapata "Ayagapata"), 25–50 CE, [Kankali Tila](/wiki/Kankali_Tila "Kankali Tila"), Mathura, [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/KHANDAGIRI_AND_UDAYGIRI_Cave_Inscriptions_1.jpg/250px-KHANDAGIRI_AND_UDAYGIRI_Cave_Inscriptions_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:KHANDAGIRI_AND_UDAYGIRI_Cave_Inscriptions_1.jpg)

Inscriptions at a [Udaygiri-Khandagiri](/wiki/Udayagiri_and_Khandagiri_Caves "Udayagiri and Khandagiri Caves") 2nd–1st-century BCE Jain rock cut cave, [Odisha](/wiki/Odisha "Odisha")[[335]](#cite_note-346)

Jainism has contributed significantly to Indian art and architecture. Jain arts depict life legends of *tirthankara* or other important people, particularly with them in a seated or standing meditative posture. *Yakshas* and *[yakshinis](/wiki/Yakshini "Yakshini")*, attendant spirits who guard the *tirthankara*, are usually shown with them.[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah1998184-347) The earliest known Jain image is in the [Patna](/wiki/Patna "Patna") museum. It is dated approximately to the third century BCE.[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah1998184-347) Bronze images of Pārśva can be seen in the Prince of Wales Museum, Mumbai, and in the Patna museum; these are dated to the second century BCE.[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUmakant_P._Shah198795-348)

*Ayagapata* is a type of [votive](/wiki/Votive "Votive") tablet used in Jainism for donation and worship in the early centuries. These tablets are decorated with objects and designs central to Jain worship such as the *[stupa](/wiki/Stupa "Stupa")*, *[dharmacakra](/wiki/Dharmacakra "Dharmacakra")* and *[triratna](/wiki/Triratna "Triratna")*. They present simultaneous trends or image and symbol worship. Numerous such stone tablets were discovered during excavations at ancient Jain sites like Kankali Tila near [Mathura](/wiki/Mathura "Mathura") in Uttar Pradesh, India. The practice of donating these tablets is documented from first century BCE to the third century CE.[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKishore201517–43-349)[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJainFischer19789–10-154) *[Samavasarana](/wiki/Samavasarana "Samavasarana")*, a preaching hall of *tirthankaras* with various beings concentrically placed, is an important theme of Jain art.[[339]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley2009184-350)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Kirti_Stambha_%28Tower_of_Fame%29_%284571936260%29.jpg/250px-Kirti_Stambha_%28Tower_of_Fame%29_%284571936260%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kirti_Stambha_(Tower_of_Fame)_(4571936260).jpg)

[Kirti Stambh](/wiki/Kirti_Stambh "Kirti Stambh") in [Chittor Fort](/wiki/Chittor_Fort "Chittor Fort"), 12th century CE

The [Jain tower in Chittor](/wiki/Kirti_Stambh "Kirti Stambh"), Rajasthan, is a good example of Jain architecture.[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOwen20121–2-351) Decorated manuscripts are preserved in Jain libraries, containing diagrams from Jain cosmology.[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah1998183-352) Most of the paintings and illustrations depict historical events, known as *Panch Kalyanaka*, from the life of the *tirthankara*. Rishabha, the first *tirthankara*, is usually depicted in either the [lotus position](/wiki/Lotus_position "Lotus position") or *kayotsarga*, the standing position. He is distinguished from other *tirthankara* by the long locks of hair falling to his shoulders. Bull images also appear in his sculptures.[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah1998113-353) In paintings, incidents from his life, like his marriage and [Indra](/wiki/Indra "Indra") marking his forehead, are depicted. Other paintings show him presenting a pottery bowl to his followers; he is also seen painting a house, weaving, and being visited by his mother Marudevi.[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJainFischer197816-354) Each of the twenty-four *tirthankara* is associated with distinctive emblems, which are listed in such texts as *Tiloyapannati*, *Kahavaali* and *Pravacanasaarodhara*.[[344]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatubhai_Shah1998187-355)

Major pilgrimage and temple sites in Jainism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Palitana.jpg/250px-Palitana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palitana.jpg)

Jain temples on [Shatrunjaya](/wiki/Shatrunjaya "Shatrunjaya") hill near [Palitana](/wiki/Palitana "Palitana"), Gujarat

A Jain temple, a *Derasar* or *Basadi*, is a [place of worship](/wiki/Place_of_worship "Place of worship").[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBabb199666-356) Temples contain *tirthankara* images, some fixed, others moveable.[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBabb199666-356) These are stationed in the inner sanctum, one of the two sacred zones, the other being the main hall.[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBabb199666-356) One of the images is marked as the *moolnayak* (primary deity).[[346]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBabb199668-357) A *[manastambha](/wiki/Manastambha "Manastambha")* (column of honor) is a pillar that is often constructed in front of Jain temples.[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESettar1989195-358) Temple construction is considered a meritorious act.[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESangave2001188-359)

Ancient Jain monuments include the Udaigiri Hills near Bhelsa ([Vidisha](/wiki/Vidisha "Vidisha")) and [Pataini temple](/wiki/Pataini_temple "Pataini temple") in Madhya Pradesh, the [Ellora](/wiki/Ellora_Caves "Ellora Caves") in Maharashtra, the [Palitana temples](/wiki/Palitana_temples "Palitana temples") in Gujarat, and the Jain temples at Dilwara Temples near [Mount Abu](/wiki/Mount_Abu "Mount Abu"), Rajasthan.[[349]](#cite_note-360)[[350]](#cite_note-361) [Chaumukha temple](/wiki/Ranakpur_Jain_temple "Ranakpur Jain temple") in [Ranakpur](/wiki/Ranakpur "Ranakpur") is considered one of the most beautiful Jain temples and is famous for its detailed carvings.[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESehdev_Kumar2001106-362) According to Jain texts, [Shikharji](/wiki/Shikharji "Shikharji") is the place where twenty of the twenty-four Jain *Tīrthaṅkaras* along with many other monks attained *moksha* (died without being reborn, with their soul in *[Siddhashila](/wiki/Siddhashila "Siddhashila")*). The Shikharji site in northeastern [Jharkhand](/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand") is therefore a revered pilgrimage site.[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2010130–133-363)[[l]](#cite_note-365) The Palitana temples are the holiest shrine for the Śvētāmbara Murtipujaka sect.[[354]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerger2010352-366) Along with Shikharji the two sites are considered the holiest of all pilgrimage sites by the [Jain community](/wiki/Jain_community "Jain community").[[355]](#cite_note-367) The [Jain complex, Khajuraho](/wiki/Jain_complex,_Khajuraho "Jain complex, Khajuraho") and [Jain Narayana temple](/wiki/Pattadakal#Jain_Temple "Pattadakal") are part of a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").[[356]](#cite_note-368)[[357]](#cite_note-369) [Shravanabelagola](/wiki/Shravanabelagola "Shravanabelagola"), [Saavira Kambada Basadi](/wiki/Saavira_Kambada_Basadi "Saavira Kambada Basadi") or *1000 pillars* and [Brahma Jinalaya](/wiki/Brahma_Jinalaya "Brahma Jinalaya") are important Jain centers in Karnataka.[[358]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEButaliaSmall2004367-370)[[359]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFergusson1876271-371)[[360]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPandya201417-372) In and around [Madurai](/wiki/Madurai "Madurai"), there are 26 caves, 200 stone beds, 60 inscriptions, and over 100 sculptures.[[361]](#cite_note-373)

The second–first century BCE Udayagiri and Khandagiri Caves are rich with carvings of *tirthanakars* and deities with inscriptions including the [Elephant Cave inscription](/wiki/Hathigumpha_inscription "Hathigumpha inscription").[[362]](#cite_note-374)[[363]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUpinder_Singh2024460-375) Jain cave temples at [Badami](/wiki/Badami_cave_temples "Badami cave temples"), [Mangi-Tungi](/wiki/Mangi-Tungi "Mangi-Tungi") and the Ellora Caves are considered important.[[364]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOwen201250-376) The [Sittanavasal Cave](/wiki/Sittanavasal_Cave "Sittanavasal Cave") temple is a fine example of Jain art with an early cave shelter, and a medieval rock-cut temple with excellent fresco paintings comparable to Ajantha. Inside are seventeen stone beds with second century BCE. [Tamil-Brahmi](/wiki/Tamil-Brahmi "Tamil-Brahmi") inscriptions.[[365]](#cite_note-377) The eighth century [Kazhugumalai temple](/wiki/Kalugumalai_Jain_Beds "Kalugumalai Jain Beds") marks the revival of Jainism in South India.[[366]](#cite_note-378)

### Pilgrimages

Main article: [Tirtha (Jainism)](/wiki/Tirtha_(Jainism) "Tirtha (Jainism)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Shikharji_Parasnath_Giridih.jpg/250px-Shikharji_Parasnath_Giridih.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shikharji_Parasnath_Giridih.jpg)

[Shikharji](/wiki/Shikharji "Shikharji")

Jain *Tirtha* (pilgrim) sites are divided into the following categories:[[367]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETitze1998-379)

* *Siddhakshetra* – Site of the [*moksha*](/wiki/Moksha_(Jainism) "Moksha (Jainism)") of an *arihant* (*kevalin*) or *tirthankara*, such as: [Ashtapada](/wiki/Mount_Kailash "Mount Kailash") of [Rishabhanatha](/wiki/Rishabhanatha "Rishabhanatha"), [Shikharji](/wiki/Shikharji "Shikharji") of 20 [Tirthankara](/wiki/Tirthankara "Tirthankara"), [Girnar](/wiki/Girnar "Girnar") of [Neminatha](/wiki/Neminatha "Neminatha"), [Pawapuri](/wiki/Pawapuri "Pawapuri") of [Mahaveera](/wiki/Mahaveera "Mahaveera"), [Champapuri](/wiki/Champapuri "Champapuri") (capital of [Anga](/wiki/Anga "Anga")) of [Vasupujya](/wiki/Vasupujya "Vasupujya"), [Mangi-Tungi](/wiki/Mangi-Tungi "Mangi-Tungi") of [Ram](/wiki/Rama "Rama"), [Palitana](/wiki/Palitana "Palitana") of 3 [Pandavas](/wiki/Pandavas "Pandavas").
* *Atishayakshetra* – Locations where divine events are believed to have occurred, such as: [Mahavirji](/wiki/Mahavirji "Mahavirji"), [Rishabhdeo](/wiki/Rishabhdeo "Rishabhdeo"), [Kundalpur](/wiki/Kundalpur "Kundalpur"), [Tijara](/wiki/Tijara_Jain_Temple "Tijara Jain Temple"), and [Aharji](/wiki/Aharji "Aharji").
* *Puranakshetra* –  Places associated with the lives of great men, such as: [Ayodhya](/wiki/Ayodhya "Ayodhya"), [Vidisha](/wiki/Vidisha "Vidisha"), [Hastinapur](/wiki/Hastinapur "Hastinapur"), and [Rajgir](/wiki/Rajgir "Rajgir").
* *Gyanakshetra* –  Places associated with famous *[acharyas](/wiki/Acharya "Acharya")*, or centers of learning, such as [Shravanabelagola](/wiki/Shravanabelagola "Shravanabelagola").

Outside contemporary India, Jain communities built [temples](/wiki/Nagarparkar_Jain_Temples "Nagarparkar Jain Temples") in locations such as [Nagarparkar](/wiki/Nagarparkar "Nagarparkar"), [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh") (Pakistan). However, according to a UNESCO tentative world heritage site application, Nagarparkar was not a "major religious centre or a place of pilgrimage" for Jainism, but it was once an important cultural landscape before "the last remaining Jain community left the area in 1947 at Partition".[[204]](#cite_note-:0-209)

### Statues and sculptures

Main article: [Jain sculpture](/wiki/Jain_sculpture "Jain sculpture")
