| [Gommateshwara statue ಗೊಮ್ಮಟೇಶ್ವರ](/wiki/File:Gommateshwara,_Shravanabelagola.jpg) The 58.8-foot high monolithic statue of [Bahubali](/wiki/Bahubali "Bahubali") built in 981 CE | |
| Religion | |
| [Affiliation](/wiki/List_of_religions_and_spiritual_traditions "List of religions and spiritual traditions") | Jainism |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Thirthankara_Suparshvanath_Museum_Rietberg_RVI_306.jpg/250px-Thirthankara_Suparshvanath_Museum_Rietberg_RVI_306.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thirthankara_Suparshvanath_Museum_Rietberg_RVI_306.jpg)

Idol of [Suparśvanātha](/wiki/Supar%C5%9Bvan%C4%81tha "Suparśvanātha")

Jain sculptures usually depict one of the twenty-four *tīrthaṅkaras*; Parshvanatha, Rishabhanatha and Mahāvīra are among the more popular, often seated in [lotus position](/wiki/Lotus_position "Lotus position") or *kayotsarga*, along with *Arihant*, [Bahubali](/wiki/Bahubali "Bahubali"), and protector deities like [Ambika](/wiki/Ambika_(Jainism) "Ambika (Jainism)").[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArora2007405-380) Quadruple images are also popular. *Tirthankar* idols look similar, differentiated by their individual symbol, except for Parshvanatha whose head is crowned by a snake. Digambara images are naked without any beautification, whereas Śvētāmbara depictions are clothed and ornamented.[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2010184-381)

A monolithic, 18-metre (59-foot) statue of Bahubali, *[Gommateshvara](/wiki/Gommateshvara "Gommateshvara")*, built in 981 CE by the [Ganga](/wiki/Western_Ganga_dynasty "Western Ganga dynasty") minister and commander [Chavundaraya](/wiki/Chavundaraya "Chavundaraya"), is situated on a hilltop in [Shravanabelagola](/wiki/Shravanabelagola "Shravanabelagola") in [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"). This statue was voted first in the SMS poll Seven Wonders of India conducted by *[The Times of India](/wiki/The_Times_of_India "The Times of India")*.[[370]](#cite_note-382) The 33-metre (108-foot) tall [Statue of Ahiṃsā](/wiki/Statue_of_Ahi%E1%B9%83s%C4%81 "Statue of Ahiṃsā") (depicting Rishabhanatha) was erected in the [Nashik district](/wiki/Nashik_district "Nashik district") in 2015.[[371]](#cite_note-383) Idols are often made in *[Ashtadhatu](/wiki/Ashtadhatu "Ashtadhatu")* (literally "eight metals"), namely [Akota Bronze](/wiki/Akota_Bronze "Akota Bronze"), [brass](/wiki/Brass "Brass"), [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold"), [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver"), stone [monoliths](/wiki/Monolith "Monolith"), [rock cut](/wiki/Rock_cut "Rock cut"), and precious stones.[[372]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPratapaditya_Pal198622-384)[[373]](#cite_note-385)

### Symbols

Main article: [Jain symbols](/wiki/Jain_symbols "Jain symbols")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Om_ja%C3%AFn_orange.svg/120px-Om_ja%C3%AFn_orange.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Om_ja%C3%AFn_orange.svg)

[Om in Jainism](/wiki/Om#Jainism "Om")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/In-jain.png/250px-In-jain.png)](/wiki/File:In-jain.png)

[Jain flag](/wiki/Jain_flag "Jain flag")

Jain icons and arts incorporate symbols such as the [swastika](/wiki/Swastika "Swastika"), [*Om*](/wiki/Om#Jainism "Om"), and the *Ashtamangala*. In Jainism, *Om* is a condensed reference to the initials "A-A-A-U-M" of the five parameshthis: "*Arihant*, *[Ashiri](/wiki/Ashiri "Ashiri")*, *[Acharya](/wiki/Acharya "Acharya")*, *[Upajjhaya](/wiki/Upajjhaya "Upajjhaya")*, *Muni*".[[374]](#cite_note-386)[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Glasenapp1925410–411-387) The *[Ashtamangala](/wiki/Ashtamangala "Ashtamangala")* is a set of eight auspicious symbols:[[376]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETitze1998234-388) in the Digambara tradition, these are [chatra](/wiki/Chatra_(umbrella) "Chatra (umbrella)"), *[dhvaja](/wiki/Dhvaja "Dhvaja")*, *[kalasha](/wiki/Kalasha "Kalasha")*, [fly-whisk](/wiki/Fly-whisk "Fly-whisk"), [mirror](/wiki/Mirror "Mirror"), chair, [hand fan](/wiki/Hand_fan "Hand fan") and vessel. In the Śvētāmbar tradition, they are Swastika, *[Srivatsa](/wiki/Srivatsa "Srivatsa")*, *[Nandavarta](/wiki/Nandavarta "Nandavarta")*, *Vardhmanaka* (food vessel), *Bhadrasana* (seat), *[Kalasha](/wiki/Kalasha "Kalasha")* (pot), *Darpan* (mirror) and pair of fish.[[376]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETitze1998234-388)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Stambha_of_Parshwanath_Jain_Mandir%2C_Belgachhiya_18.jpg/250px-Stambha_of_Parshwanath_Jain_Mandir%2C_Belgachhiya_18.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stambha_of_Parshwanath_Jain_Mandir,_Belgachhiya_18.jpg)

A symbol to represent the Jain community was chosen in 1975 as part of the commemoration of the 2,500th anniversary of Mahavira's *nirvana*.

The hand with a wheel on the palm symbolizes [*ahimsā*](/wiki/Ahimsa_in_Jainism "Ahimsa in Jainism"). The wheel represents the *[dharmachakra](/wiki/Dharmachakra "Dharmachakra")*, which stands for the resolve to halt the *saṃsāra* (wandering) through the relentless pursuit of *ahimsā*. The five colours of the [Jain flag](/wiki/Jain_flag "Jain flag") represent the *[Pañca-Parameṣṭhi](/wiki/Pa%C3%B1ca-Parame%E1%B9%A3%E1%B9%ADhi "Pañca-Parameṣṭhi")* and the five vows.[[377]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVijay_K._Jain2012iv-389) The [swastika](/wiki/Swastika "Swastika")'s four arms symbolise the four realms in which rebirth occurs according to Jainism: humans, heavenly beings, hellish beings and non-humans.[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a17-390)[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJansmaJain2006123-391) The three dots on the top represent the three jewels mentioned in ancient texts: correct faith, correct understanding and correct conduct, believed to lead to spiritual perfection.[[380]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort2001a17–18-392)

In 1974, on the 2500th anniversary of the nirvana of Mahāvīra, the Jain community chose a single combined image for Jainism.[[381]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobinson2006225-393) It depicts the three *lokas*, heaven, the human world and hell. The semi-circular topmost portion symbolizes *Siddhashila*, a zone beyond the three realms. The Jain swastika and the symbol of *Ahiṃsā* are included, with the Jain mantra *[Parasparopagraho Jīvānām](/wiki/Parasparopagraho_Jivanam "Parasparopagraho Jivanam")*[[382]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESangave2001123-394) from sūtra 5.21 of Umaswati's *Tattvarthasūtra*, meaning "souls render service to one another".[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVallely2013358-395)

## Jainism and ecology

Jainism, one of the world's oldest religions, offers a profound ecological philosophy rooted in its core principles.[[384]](#cite_note-JE-396) As noted in the Jain Declaration on Nature, "Jainism is fundamentally a religion of ecology and has turned ecology into a religion. It has enabled Jains to create an environment-friendly value system and code of conduct."[[385]](#cite_note-YA-397) Central to Jain ethics is *[ahimsa](/wiki/Ahimsa "Ahimsa")* (non-violence), which extends beyond human interactions to encompass all living beings. "All breathing, existing, living, sentient beings should not be slain, nor treated with violence, nor abused, nor tormented, nor driven away. This is the pure, unchangeable, eternal law."[[386]](#cite_note-398)

The Jain concept of *parasparopagrahojīvānām* in the [Tattvarth Sutra](/wiki/Tattvarth_Sutra "Tattvarth Sutra"), the most authoritative sacred text of Jains, teaches that all souls are responsible for one another and underscores the mutual interdependence of all life forms.[[387]](#cite_note-GO-399) [Mahavira](/wiki/Mahavira "Mahavira"), the founder of Jainism taught, "One who neglects or disregards the existence of earth, air, fire, water and vegetation disregards his own existence which is entwined with them."[[388]](#cite_note-FOR-400) This principle is not merely philosophical but is reflected in daily practices. For instance, Jain monks and nuns often wear masks to prevent inhaling and harming microscopic organisms, demonstrating meticulous care for even the smallest forms of life.[[389]](#cite_note-401)

Jainism also emphasizes *[aparigraha](/wiki/Aparigraha "Aparigraha")* (non-possessiveness), advocating for minimal consumption and a lifestyle that avoids excess. This principle encourages individuals to live sustainably, reducing their ecological footprint. "Using any resource beyond one's needs and misuse of any part of nature is considered a form of theft. Indeed, the Jain faith goes one radical step further and declares unequivocally that waste and creating pollution are acts of violence."[[390]](#cite_note-402)

In contemporary times, Jain communities continue to uphold these ecological principles through various initiatives. These include [tree planting](/wiki/Tree_planting "Tree planting"), [wildlife conservation](/wiki/Wildlife_conservation "Wildlife conservation"), and promoting [vegetarianism](/wiki/Vegetarianism "Vegetarianism"), all aimed at fostering a harmonious relationship with nature. Such practices exemplify the enduring relevance of Jain teachings in addressing modern environmental challenges.[[391]](#cite_note-403)

Through its unwavering commitment to non-violence, interdependence, and minimalism, Jainism offers a holistic framework for ecological sustainability, emphasizing the sacredness of all life and the importance of living in harmony with the environment.[[392]](#cite_note-404)

## Comparison with other religions

Main articles: [Buddhism and Jainism](/wiki/Buddhism_and_Jainism "Buddhism and Jainism") and [Jainism and Hinduism](/wiki/Jainism_and_Hinduism "Jainism and Hinduism")

Jain stupa

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Holi_relief%2C_Mathura%2C_c1st_century_CE.jpg/330px-Holi_relief%2C_Mathura%2C_c1st_century_CE.jpg)](/wiki/File:Holi_relief,_Mathura,_c1st_century_CE.jpg)

Jain votive plaque with [Jain stupa](/wiki/Jain_stupa "Jain stupa"), the "Vasu Śilāpaṭa" ayagapata, 1st century CE, excavated from [Kankali Tila](/wiki/Kankali_Tila "Kankali Tila"), [Mathura](/wiki/Mathura "Mathura").[[393]](#cite_note-405)  
The inscription reads:  
*"Adoration to the Arhat Vardhamana. The daughter of the matron (?) courtesan Lonasobhika (Lavanasobhika), the disciple of the ascetics, the junior (?) courtesan Vasu has erected a shrine of the Arhat, a hall of homage (ayagasabha), cistern and a stone slab at the sanctuary of the [Nirgrantha](/wiki/Nirgrantha "Nirgrantha") Arhats, together with her mother, her daughter, her son and her whole household in honour of the Arhats."*[[394]](#cite_note-406)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Sculpture_panel_showing_a_Jain_stupa_and_torana%2C_Mathura_75-100_CE.jpg/330px-Sculpture_panel_showing_a_Jain_stupa_and_torana%2C_Mathura_75-100_CE.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sculpture_panel_showing_a_Jain_stupa_and_torana,_Mathura_75-100_CE.jpg)

*Sivayasa Ayagapata*, with stupa fragment, [Kankali Tila](/wiki/Kankali_Tila "Kankali Tila"), 75–100 CE

All four [Dharmic religions](/wiki/Indian_religions "Indian religions")—Jainism, [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism")—share concepts and doctrines such as [karma](/wiki/Karma "Karma") and [rebirth](/wiki/Reincarnation "Reincarnation").[[395]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESolomonHiggins199811–22-407)[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAppleton20161–21,_25–27,_57–58,_82–84-408)[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcFaul200627–28-409) They do not believe in eternal [heaven](/wiki/Heaven "Heaven") or [hell](/wiki/Hell "Hell") or [judgment day](/wiki/Judgment_day "Judgment day"), and leave it up to individual discretion to choose whether or not to believe in gods, to disagree with core teachings, and to choose whether to participate in prayers, rituals and festivals. They all consider values such as *[ahimsa](/wiki/Ahimsa "Ahimsa")* (non-violence) to be important,[[398]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShawDemy2017-410): p. 635 link suffering to craving, individual's actions, intents, and karma, and believe spirituality is a means to enlightened peace, bliss and eternal liberation (*[moksha](/wiki/Moksha "Moksha")*).[[399]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESolomonHiggins199818–22-411)[[400]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcFaul200627–40-412)

Jainism differs from both Buddhism and Hinduism in its ontological premises: While all three believe in impermanence, Buddhism incorporates the premise of *[anatta](/wiki/Anatta "Anatta")* ("no eternal self or soul") while Hinduism maintains the concept of an eternal unchanging *[atman](/wiki/Atman_(Hinduism) "Atman (Hinduism)")* ("soul"); by contrast, Jainism incorporates an eternal but changing *[jiva](/wiki/Jiva_(Jainism) "Jiva (Jainism)")* ("soul").[[401]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas200287–88-413)[[402]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley20042–5-414)[[403]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2013122–125-415) In Jain thought, there are infinite eternal *jivas*, predominantly in cycles of rebirth, and a few *siddhas* (liberated ones).[[404]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHiriyanna1993157–158,_168–169-416) Unlike Jainism, Hindu philosophies encompass [nondualism](/wiki/Advaita "Advaita") where all souls are identical as Brahman and posited as interconnected one[[405]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHiriyanna199354–62,_77–82,_132-417)[[406]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerrett2013247–248-418)[[407]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartley20131–10,_76–79,_87–98-419) Jainism rejected the non-dual concept, stating that if there were only one universal consciousness which was already liberated, the purpose of dharma would be nullified. Additionally, the need and desire for an infinitely blissful consciousness to create the universe would imply a limitation within that consciousness. Jainism also criticized Vedanta's inability to explain how an intangible consciousness could create a material universe, filled with countless living beings who experience suffering.[[408]](#cite_note-420) Jain scholar Dr. Hukumchand Bharill explains that, according to both Jainism and Vedanta, only consciousness can perceive itself, while the [mind](/wiki/Mind "Mind") and [body](/wiki/Human_body "Human body") are incapable of recognizing and experiencing the soul. In Jainism, the soul, in its state of ignorance, mistakenly identifies with the body and consequently experiences suffering. When the soul realizes its true nature, it attains enlightenment, gaining infinite knowledge and bliss. If there were only a singular, universal consciousness, Bharill questions, who attains realization as the consciousness is already liberated, and the mind is incapable of experiencing soul's boundless knowledge-bliss nature.[[409]](#cite_note-421)

While both Hinduism and Jainism believe "soul exists" to be a self-evident truth, most Hindu systems consider it to be eternally present, infinite and constant (*[vibhu](/w/index.php?title=Vibhu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vibhu (page does not exist)")*), but some Hindu scholars propose soul to be atomic. Hindu thought generally discusses Atman and [Brahman](/wiki/Brahman "Brahman") through a monistic or dualistic framework. In contrast, Jain thought denies the Hindu metaphysical concept of Brahman, and Jain philosophy considers the soul to be ever changing and bound to the body or matter for each lifetime, thereby having a finite size that infuses the entire body of a living being.[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini199858,_102–105-422)

Jainism is similar to Buddhism in not recognizing the primacy of the Vedas and the Hindu Brahman. Jainism and Hinduism, however, both believe "soul exists" as a self-evident truth.[[399]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESolomonHiggins199818–22-411)[[411]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010b174–175-423) Jains and Hindus have frequently intermarried, particularly in northern, central and western regions of India.[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJuergensmeyer201154-424)[[413]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelting2009206_note_4-425) Some early colonial scholars stated that Jainism like Buddhism was, in part, a rejection of the Hindu caste system,[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENesfield1885116–117-426)[[415]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPope188040–41-427) but later scholars consider this a Western error.[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlberts2007258–259-428) A caste system not based on birth has been a historic part of Jain society, and Jainism focused on transforming the individual, not society.[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJuergensmeyer201154-424)[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002147–149,_304_footnote_24-429)[[418]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBabb1996137–145,_54,_172-430)[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESangave198073,_316–317-431)[[m]](#cite_note-434)

Monasticism is similar in all three traditions,[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnston2000681–683-435)[[423]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaillat2003a30–34_with_footnote_28-436) with similar rules, hierarchical structure, not traveling during the four-month [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") season, and celibacy,[[423]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaillat2003a30–34_with_footnote_28-436) originating before the Buddha or the Mahāvīra.[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnston2000681–683-435) Jain and Hindu monastic communities have traditionally been more mobile and had an itinerant lifestyle, while Buddhist monks have favored belonging to a *sangha* (monastery) and staying in its premises.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirakawa19934–7-143) Buddhist monastic rules forbid a monk to go outside without wearing the sangha's distinctive ruddy robe, or to use wooden bowls.[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnston2000681–683-435) In contrast, Jain monastic rules have either required nakedness (Digambara) or white clothes (Śvētāmbara), and they have disagreed on the legitimacy of the wooden or empty gourd as the begging bowl by Jain monks.[[422]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnston2000681–683-435)[[n]](#cite_note-438)

Jains have similar views with Hindus that violence in self-defence can be justified,[[425]](#cite_note-439) and that a soldier who kills enemies in combat is performing a legitimate duty.[[426]](#cite_note-440) Jain communities accepted the use of military power for their defence; there were Jain monarchs, military commanders, and soldiers.[[427]](#cite_note-441) The Jain and Hindu communities have often been very close and mutually accepting. Some Hindu temples have included a Jain *Tirthankara* within its premises in a place of honour,[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong20135–6-442)[[429]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharmaGhosal2006100–103-443) while temple complexes such as the [Badami cave temples](/wiki/Badami_cave_temples "Badami cave temples") and [Khajuraho](/wiki/Khajuraho "Khajuraho") feature both Hindu and Jain monuments.[[430]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichell201438–52,_60–61-444)[[431]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERingWatsonSchellinger1996468–470-445)

Fynes (1996) argues that various Jain influences, particularly ideas on the existence of [plant souls](/wiki/Plant_soul "Plant soul"), were transmitted from [Western Kshatrapa](/wiki/Western_Satraps "Western Satraps") territories to [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") and then integrated into [Manichaean](/wiki/Manichaeism "Manichaeism") beliefs.[[432]](#cite_note-FynesRCC-446)

## Demographics

Main article: [Jain communities](/wiki/Jain_communities "Jain communities")

With an estimated four to five million followers worldwide,[[433]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVoorst201496-447)[[434]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeltonBaumann2010lix,_1395-448) the vast majority of Jains reside in India. According to the [2011 Census of India](/wiki/2011_Census_of_India "2011 Census of India"), there were 4.45 million Jains in the country, constituting 0.37% of the total population.[[435]](#cite_note-jaindemographics-449) The community is highly concentrated, with 78.2% of Indian Jains living in four states: [Maharashtra](/wiki/Jainism_in_Maharashtra "Jainism in Maharashtra") (31.5%), [Rajasthan](/wiki/Jainism_in_Rajasthan "Jainism in Rajasthan") (14.0%), [Gujarat](/wiki/Jainism_in_Gujarat "Jainism in Gujarat") (13.0%) and [Madhya Pradesh](/wiki/Madhya_Pradesh "Madhya Pradesh") (12.7%).[[435]](#cite_note-jaindemographics-449) Other states with significant populations include [Karnataka](/wiki/Jainism_in_Karnataka "Jainism in Karnataka") (9.9%), [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Jainism_in_Uttar_Pradesh "Jainism in Uttar Pradesh") (4.8%), [Delhi](/wiki/Jainism_in_Delhi "Jainism in Delhi") (3.7%) and [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Jain "Tamil Jain") (2.0%).[[435]](#cite_note-jaindemographics-449) In 2014, the [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") granted Jainism "national minority" status.

### Global diaspora

Significant Jain communities exist globally, largely tracing their origins to Indian migration.[[436]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200943-450) The oldest of these is in [East Africa](/wiki/Jainism_in_Africa "Jainism in Africa"), particularly Kenya and Uganda, where Jain merchants settled in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.[[437]](#cite_note-451) Political instability in those countries in the 1960s and 1970s led to a secondary migration, primarily to the [United Kingdom](/wiki/Jainism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Jainism in the United Kingdom"), which now has a community estimated at 25,000 to 30,000.[[438]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMugambi2010108-452) The first Jain temple consecrated outside India was in [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester "Leicester").[[438]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMugambi2010108-452)

The largest diaspora community is in the [United States](/wiki/Jainism_in_the_United_States "Jainism in the United States"), with estimates ranging from 80,000 to 100,000, and a significant population also resides in [Canada](/wiki/Jainism_in_Canada "Jainism in Canada") (est. 12,000+).[[436]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley200943-450) A notable community exists in Antwerp, Belgium, where Jains have played a prominent role in the global diamond trade since the mid-20th century. In recent decades, Jainism has also attracted converts in other nations, such as [Japan](/wiki/Jainism_in_Japan "Jainism in Japan").[[439]](#cite_note-453)[[440]](#cite_note-454)

### Socio-economic profile

The Jain community is a distinct socio-economic group within India. Data from the 2015–16 [National Family Health Survey](/wiki/National_Family_Health_Survey "National Family Health Survey") (NFHS-4) identified Jains as the wealthiest religious community in the country.[[441]](#cite_note-455) This is strongly correlated with high educational attainment. According to the 2011 census, Jains have the highest literacy rate in India at 86.7% (for ages 7+), and this figure exceeded 97% for the population excluding the retired.[[442]](#cite_note-456) Jains also have the highest percentage of college graduates of any religious community.[[443]](#cite_note-457)

Scholars often attribute this socio-economic profile to the theological principles of the faith.[[444]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESangave1980277-458) The rigorous observance of *Ahiṃsā* (non-violence) historically discouraged professions like agriculture, which involve the harming of insects and microbes.[[445]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESangave1980278-459)[[446]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002191-460) This steered the community toward mercantile pursuits, such as commerce, banking, jewelry, and trade.[[446]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002191-460)[[447]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESangave1980279-461) A notable historical example of this financial prominence was the [Jagat Seth](/wiki/Jagat_Seth "Jagat Seth") family, a Jain banking house that served as state bankers to the [Nawabs of Bengal](/wiki/Nawabs_of_Bengal "Nawabs of Bengal") and the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") in the 18th century.[[448]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalrymple2019-462)[[449]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamkrishna_Mukherjee1955239-463)

In the modern era, this focus has translated to high representation in business, finance, and professional fields.[[446]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002191-460)[[447]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESangave1980279-461)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998177–178-33) Foundational industrialists like [Walchand Hirachand](/wiki/Walchand_Hirachand "Walchand Hirachand"), who established India's first modern shipyard and aircraft factory, emerged from the community.[[450]](#cite_note-464) In finance, the merchant [Premchand Roychand](/wiki/Premchand_Roychand "Premchand Roychand") was a key 19th-century founder of the [Bombay Stock Exchange](/wiki/Bombay_Stock_Exchange "Bombay Stock Exchange").[[451]](#cite_note-465) This prominence in large-scale industry continues with contemporary figures such as [Gautam Adani](/wiki/Gautam_Adani "Gautam Adani"), founder of the [Adani Group](/wiki/Adani_Group "Adani Group"), and the Patni family of [Kishangarh](/wiki/Kishangarh "Kishangarh"), founders of RK Marble, a major global stone processor. This representation also extends to technology and the sciences. [Narendra Patni](/wiki/Narendra_Patni "Narendra Patni"), for instance, was a pioneer of the Indian IT services industry with the founding of [Patni Computer Systems](/wiki/Patni_Computer_Systems "Patni Computer Systems"). In science, physicist [Vikram Sarabhai](/wiki/Vikram_Sarabhai "Vikram Sarabhai"), founder of the [Indian Space Research Organisation](/wiki/Indian_Space_Research_Organisation "Indian Space Research Organisation") (ISRO), is widely regarded as the "father of the Indian space program."[[452]](#cite_note-466)

### Community and philanthropy

A defining characteristic of the Jain community is its highly organized and historical practice of *dāna* (charity).[[453]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002197-467) This is a central religious duty for laypersons (*śrāvakas*), who are motivated by two key theological concepts.[[453]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002197-467) Firstly, scholars identify *dāna* as the primary mechanism for the laity to acquire good *karma* (*punya*), which is sought to ensure worldly well-being and a favorable rebirth. Secondly, this practice is the main expression of the householder's vow of *Aparigraha* (non-attachment). This dual motivation has resulted in a long-standing tradition of institutional philanthropy.[[453]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002197-467)

This is most famously expressed in the establishment of *panjrapoles* (animal hospitals and sanctuaries), which provide care for animals in accordance with the principle of *Ahiṃsā*.[[454]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002197–198-468) The community has also historically funded and managed *dharamshalas* (pilgrim rest-houses), educational institutions, and *bhandaras* (manuscript libraries), which have been crucial in preserving tens of thousands of ancient and medieval texts.[[454]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002197–198-468)

In the 20th century, this philanthropic tradition of supporting educational institutions evolved to include the founding of major national bodies. Industrialist [Kasturbhai Lalbhai](/wiki/Kasturbhai_Lalbhai "Kasturbhai Lalbhai"), for example, was a key co-founder of the [Indian Institute of Management, Ahmedabad](/wiki/Indian_Institute_of_Management,_Ahmedabad "Indian Institute of Management, Ahmedabad") (IIM-A) and the [Physical Research Laboratory](/wiki/Physical_Research_Laboratory "Physical Research Laboratory"). In the field of public policy, physicist [Daulat Singh Kothari](/wiki/Daulat_Singh_Kothari "Daulat Singh Kothari") chaired the influential [Kothari Commission](/wiki/Kothari_Commission "Kothari Commission") (1964–1966), which provided the comprehensive framework for the modernization of India's education policy.

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** According to Padmanabh S. Jaini, *samyak darśana* is acceptance of the truth of soul (*jīva*).[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998141–147-17)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-45)** This view, however, is not shared by all Jain sub-traditions. For example, the Terapanthi Jain tradition, with about 250,000 followers, considers both good karma such as compassionate charity, and bad karma such as sin, as binding one's soul to worldly morality. It states that any karma leads to a negation of the "absolute non-violence" principle, given man's limited perspective. It recommends that the monk or nun seeking salvation must avoid hurting or helping any being in any form.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlügel20021266–1267-44)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-57)** Jain literature, like Buddhist and Hindu literature, has also debated the aspects of violence and non-violence in food creation.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlson20141–7-56)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-62)** The five vows are: *ahiṃsā* (non-violence), *[satya](/wiki/Satya "Satya")* (truth), *[asteya](/wiki/Achourya "Achourya")* (not stealing), *[brahmacharya](/wiki/Brahmacharya "Brahmacharya")* (chastity), and *aparigraha* (non-possessiveness).
5. **[^](#cite_ref-130)** Long notes that [Ṛṣabha](/w/index.php?title=%E1%B9%9A%E1%B9%A3abha&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ṛṣabha (page does not exist)"), the first Tīrthaṅkara of Jainism, means "bull," and that images of bulls are found at seals from the [Indus Valley civilization](/wiki/Indus_Valley_civilization "Indus Valley civilization"), speculating that they may be related to Jainism.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong201353–54-129)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-237)** According to Long, this view shows influence from Buddhism and Vedanta, which see bondage are arising from [avidya](/wiki/Avidy%C4%81_(Buddhism) "Avidyā (Buddhism)"), ignorance, and see the ultimate solution to this in a form of spiritual [gnosis](/wiki/Gnosis "Gnosis").[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2013127-234) Johnson also notes that "his use of a vyavahara/niscaya distinction [...] has more in common with Madhyamaka Buddhism and even more with Advaita Vedanta than with the Jain philosophy of Anekantavada."[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson1995137-235) Cort, referring to Johnson, notes that "a minority position exemplified by Kundakunda has deemphasized conduct and focused upon knowledge alone."[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECort199810-236)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-273)** In Jainism, the *ahiṃsā* precept for a mendicant requires avoidance of touching or disturbing any living being including plants. It also mandates never swimming in water, nor lighting or fire or extinguish one, nor thrashing arms in the air as such actions can torment or hurt other beings that live in those states of matter.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor2008892–894-51)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-283)** The first is desavakasika (staying in a restrained surrounding, cutting down worldly activities). The third is *posadhopavasa* (fasting on the 8th and 14th days on lunar waxing and waning cycles). The fourth is *dana* (giving alms to Jain monks, nuns or spiritual people).[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaini1998180–181-282)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-289)** According to Dundas, *samayika* seems to have meant "correct behavior" in early Jainism.[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDundas2002170-288)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-336)** Not to be confused with the four *[Vedas](/wiki/Veda "Veda")* of Hinduism.[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal2010a164–165-335)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-341)** That Which Is, known as the *Tattvartha Sūtra* to Jains, is recognized by all four Jain traditions as the earliest, most authoritative and comprehensive summary of their religion."[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUmāsvāti1994xi–xiii-340)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-365)** Some texts refer to the place as Mount Sammeta.[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacobi1964275-364)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-434)** According to [Richard Gombrich](/wiki/Richard_Gombrich "Richard Gombrich") and other scholars, Buddhism too was not a rejection or rebellion against any ancient caste system and it too was focused on individual's liberation from rebirths and suffering. The caste system in Buddhist societies and monasteries outside India have been documented. Gombrich states, "Some modernists go so far as to say that the Buddha was against caste altogether: this is not the case, but is one of the mistakes picked up from western authors."[[420]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGombrich2012344–353_with_footnotes-432)[[416]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlberts2007258–259-428)[[421]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlorida2005134–137-433)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-438)** Whether the begging utensils of a monk, such as robe and begging bowl, were justified and legitimate for a Jain monk and were not considered an impediment on the path to salvation, remained a bone of contention among various splinter groups within Jainism, and was partly responsible for the ultimate Digambara-Svetambara split, although it would be a grave oversimplification to reduce the roots of the split to just monks' robes and bowls.[[424]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBalcerowicz201542–43-437)
